title: Ellipse

infobox.value: is a [plane curve](/wiki/Plane_curve "Plane curve") surrounding two [focal points](/wiki/Focus_(geometry) "Focus (geometry)"), such that for all points on the curve, the sum of both distances to the two focal points is a constant. It generalizes a [circle](/wiki/Circle "Circle"), which is the special type of ellipse in which the two focal points are the same. The elongation of an ellipse is measured by its [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(mathematics) "Eccentricity (mathematics)") ![{\displaystyle e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd253103f0876afc68ebead27a5aa9867d927467), a number ranging from ![{\displaystyle e=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d9850169d70a5ab7df71c2126441a86cec93eec8) (the [limiting case](/wiki/Limiting_case_(mathematics) "Limiting case (mathematics)") of a circle) to ![{\displaystyle e=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c2f5932668126c63c844dc00ca187bc58a29e5a) (the limiting case of infinite elongation, no longer an ellipse but a [parabola](/wiki/Parabola "Parabola")).

This article is about the geometric figure. For other uses, see [Ellipse (disambiguation)](/wiki/Ellipse_(disambiguation) "Ellipse (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [ellipsis](/wiki/Ellipsis "Ellipsis"), [eclipse](/wiki/Eclipse "Eclipse"), or [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Ellipse-conic.svg/250px-Ellipse-conic.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-conic.svg)

An ellipse (red) obtained as the intersection of a cone with an inclined plane.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Ellipse-var.svg/250px-Ellipse-var.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-var.svg)

Ellipses: examples with increasing eccentricity

In [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), an **ellipse** is a [plane curve](/wiki/Plane_curve "Plane curve") surrounding two [focal points](/wiki/Focus_(geometry) "Focus (geometry)"), such that for all points on the curve, the sum of both distances to the two focal points is a constant. It generalizes a [circle](/wiki/Circle "Circle"), which is the special type of ellipse in which the two focal points are the same. The elongation of an ellipse is measured by its [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(mathematics) "Eccentricity (mathematics)") ![{\displaystyle e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd253103f0876afc68ebead27a5aa9867d927467), a number ranging from ![{\displaystyle e=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d9850169d70a5ab7df71c2126441a86cec93eec8) (the [limiting case](/wiki/Limiting_case_(mathematics) "Limiting case (mathematics)") of a circle) to ![{\displaystyle e=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c2f5932668126c63c844dc00ca187bc58a29e5a) (the limiting case of infinite elongation, no longer an ellipse but a [parabola](/wiki/Parabola "Parabola")).

An ellipse has a simple [algebraic](/wiki/Algebra "Algebra") formula for its area, but for [its perimeter](/wiki/Perimeter_of_an_ellipse "Perimeter of an ellipse") (also known as [circumference](/wiki/Circumference "Circumference")), [integration](/wiki/Integral "Integral") is required to obtain an exact solution.

The largest and smallest [diameters](/wiki/Diameter "Diameter") of an ellipse, also known as its width and height, are typically denoted 2a and 2b. An ellipse has four [extreme points](/wiki/Extreme_point "Extreme point"): two *[vertices](/wiki/Vertex_(geometry) "Vertex (geometry)")* at the endpoints of the [major axis](/wiki/Major_axis "Major axis") and two *co-vertices* at the endpoints of the minor axis.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Ellipse-def0.svg/250px-Ellipse-def0.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-def0.svg)

Notable points and line segments in an ellipse.

[Analytically](/wiki/Analytic_geometry "Analytic geometry"), the equation of a standard ellipse centered at the origin is:
![{\displaystyle {\frac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/863293f65080f512375d767be3a54281b1b5c2b2)
Assuming ![{\displaystyle a\geq b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ed5d3957d5f94566507526017e4ebb67c02efe81), the foci are ![{\displaystyle (\pm c,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0ee81878c5a2a16689433b7274a67ab3bf6e3762), where ![{\textstyle c={\sqrt {a^{2}-b^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5db78643238b44e5f288d9b0b3c996e3e69a44a5) (the [*linear eccentricity*](#Linear_eccentricity)) is the distance from the center to a focus. The standard [parametrization](/wiki/Parametrization "Parametrization") is:
![{\displaystyle (x,y)=(a\cos(t),b\sin(t))\quad {\text{for}}\quad 0\leq t\leq 2\pi .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/379e0f793358b2105a5fc0a23cac22fe16fd76ce)

Ellipses are the [closed](/wiki/Closed_curve "Closed curve") type of [conic section](/wiki/Conic_section "Conic section"): a plane curve tracing the intersection of a [cone](/wiki/Cone "Cone") with a [plane](/wiki/Plane_(mathematics) "Plane (mathematics)") (see figure). Ellipses have many similarities with the other two forms of conic sections, parabolas and [hyperbolas](/wiki/Hyperbola "Hyperbola"), both of which are [open](/wiki/Open_curve "Open curve") and [unbounded](/wiki/Unbounded_set "Unbounded set"). An angled [cross section](/wiki/Cross_section_(geometry) "Cross section (geometry)") of a right circular [cylinder](/wiki/Cylinder_(geometry)#Cylindric_section "Cylinder (geometry)") is also an ellipse.

An ellipse may also be defined in terms of one focal point and a line outside the ellipse called the [*directrix*](#Eccentricity_and_the_directrix_property): for all points on the ellipse, the ratio between the distance to the focus and the distance to the directrix is a constant, called the [*eccentricity*](#Eccentricity_and_the_directrix_property):
![{\displaystyle e={\frac {c}{a}}={\sqrt {1-{\frac {b^{2}}{a^{2}}}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/74cfe003f7f80d258931c31b5d4bd2ccfba2e24e)

Ellipses are common in [physics](/wiki/Physics "Physics"), [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy") and [engineering](/wiki/Engineering "Engineering"). For example, the [orbit](/wiki/Kepler_orbit "Kepler orbit") of each planet in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System") is approximately an ellipse with the Sun at one focus point (more precisely, the focus is the [barycenter](/wiki/Barycentric_coordinates_(astronomy) "Barycentric coordinates (astronomy)") of the Sun–planet pair). The same is true for moons orbiting planets and all other systems of two astronomical bodies. The shapes of planets and stars are often well described by [ellipsoids](/wiki/Ellipsoid "Ellipsoid"). A circle viewed from a side angle looks like an ellipse: that is, the ellipse is the image of a circle under [parallel](/wiki/Parallel_projection "Parallel projection") or [perspective projection](/wiki/Perspective_projection "Perspective projection"). The ellipse is also the simplest [Lissajous figure](/wiki/Lissajous_figure "Lissajous figure") formed when the horizontal and vertical motions are [sinusoids](/wiki/Sine_wave "Sine wave") with the same frequency: a similar effect leads to [elliptical polarization](/wiki/Elliptical_polarization "Elliptical polarization") of light in [optics](/wiki/Optics "Optics").

The name, ἔλλειψις (*élleipsis*, "omission"), was given by [Apollonius of Perga](/wiki/Apollonius_of_Perga "Apollonius of Perga") in his *Conics*.

## Definition as locus of points

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition as locus of points")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Ellipse-def-e.svg/250px-Ellipse-def-e.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-def-e.svg)

Ellipse: definition by sum of distances to foci

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Ellipse-def-dc.svg/250px-Ellipse-def-dc.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-def-dc.svg)

Ellipse: definition by focus and circular directrix

An ellipse can be defined geometrically as a set or [locus of points](/wiki/Locus_of_points "Locus of points") in the Euclidean plane:

Given two fixed points ![{\displaystyle F_{1},F_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ddbaefad7d000285a069031a623cdc454e4b79e4) called the foci and a distance ![{\displaystyle 2a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d325c24be7d760207674a169b078892bdd5cbc76) which is greater than the distance between the foci, the ellipse is the set of points ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) such that the sum of the distances ![{\displaystyle |PF_{1}|,\ |PF_{2}|}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/11f9cd6112506b260df20f12b6c45035dee7692d) is equal to ![{\displaystyle 2a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d325c24be7d760207674a169b078892bdd5cbc76): ![{\displaystyle E=\left\{P\in \mathbb {R} ^{2}\,\mid \,\left|PF_{2}\right|+\left|PF_{1}\right|=2a\right\}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bdc50045c00f914dd8fffac2bd88d4932ca12876)

The midpoint ![{\displaystyle C}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4fc55753007cd3c18576f7933f6f089196732029) of the [line segment](/wiki/Line_segment "Line segment") joining the foci is called the *center* of the ellipse. The line through the foci is called the *major axis*, and the line perpendicular to it through the center is the *minor axis*. The major axis intersects the ellipse at two *[vertices](/wiki/Vertex_(curve) "Vertex (curve)")* ![{\displaystyle V_{1},V_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/50193d5201b7ff4d46487f5d5e58ee9c1e6f29fc), which have distance ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) to the center. The distance ![{\displaystyle c}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/86a67b81c2de995bd608d5b2df50cd8cd7d92455) of the foci to the center is called the *focal distance* or linear eccentricity. The quotient ![{\displaystyle e={\tfrac {c}{a}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9eecd5578dc51638b2b9d79cf72d70b98cbb4197) is defined as the *eccentricity*.

The case ![{\displaystyle F_{1}=F_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4a1387d67417e45de53b554e8b775be7ad3cb236) yields a circle and is included as a special type of ellipse.

The equation ![{\displaystyle \left|PF_{2}\right|+\left|PF_{1}\right|=2a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f37d94229edd8c50e32aa06a23356b7d97ae9d4f) can be viewed in a different way (see figure):

If ![{\displaystyle c_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0b30ba1b247fb8d334580cec68561e749d24aff2) is the circle with center ![{\displaystyle F_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0fd17e0779153d765b40ebef91533489b87b2e37) and radius ![{\displaystyle 2a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d325c24be7d760207674a169b078892bdd5cbc76), then the distance of a point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) to the circle ![{\displaystyle c_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0b30ba1b247fb8d334580cec68561e749d24aff2) equals the distance to the focus ![{\displaystyle F_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/100c7fbf174fe8b06eacc2a6b0bb2e1badd1c7ce): ![{\displaystyle \left|PF_{1}\right|=\left|Pc_{2}\right|.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6a60747a23bdec8c1654b53f121edfb28fc249b2)

![{\displaystyle c_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0b30ba1b247fb8d334580cec68561e749d24aff2) is called the *circular directrix* (related to focus ![{\displaystyle F_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0fd17e0779153d765b40ebef91533489b87b2e37)) of the ellipse.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) This property should not be confused with the definition of an ellipse using a directrix line below.

Using [Dandelin spheres](/wiki/Dandelin_spheres "Dandelin spheres"), one can prove that any section of a cone with a plane is an ellipse, assuming the plane does not contain the apex and has slope less than that of the lines on the cone.

## In Cartesian coordinates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: In Cartesian coordinates")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Ellipse-param.svg/250px-Ellipse-param.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-param.svg)

Shape parameters:

* *a*: semi-major axis,
* *b*: semi-minor axis,
* *c*: linear eccentricity,
* *p*: semi-latus rectum (usually ![{\displaystyle \ell }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f066e981e530bacc07efc6a10fa82deee985929e)).

### Standard equation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Standard equation")]

The standard form of an ellipse in Cartesian coordinates assumes that the origin is the center of the ellipse, the *x*-axis is the major axis, and:

* the foci are the points ![{\displaystyle F_{1}=(c,\,0),\ F_{2}=(-c,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/04a009dc92840e710212588725cf6ebaa3a7b8c9),
* the vertices are ![{\displaystyle V_{1}=(a,\,0),\ V_{2}=(-a,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/912aff439ff541eb2fe65a7ca6fd38d1a6d9fdca).

For an arbitrary point ![{\displaystyle (x,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/41cf50e4a314ca8e2c30964baa8d26e5be7a9386) the distance to the focus ![{\displaystyle (c,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fde1d2813d28b59afd35f5e3eb2261f647fa8556) is ![{\textstyle {\sqrt {(x-c)^{2}+y^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c5e604dc5bf697660850c50351225926a182de04) and to the other focus ![{\textstyle {\sqrt {(x+c)^{2}+y^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5b4360fe1fc0926541401374a50ba6605da77d58). Hence the point ![{\displaystyle (x,\,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0e7afe5870de593e694568d1c4b0b95c6ac50777) is on the ellipse whenever:
![{\displaystyle {\sqrt {(x-c)^{2}+y^{2}}}+{\sqrt {(x+c)^{2}+y^{2}}}=2a\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e15f97cb735b9a82d625eeaadefff259768024d8)

Removing the [radicals](/wiki/Radical_expression "Radical expression") by suitable squarings and using ![{\displaystyle b^{2}=a^{2}-c^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/de9c4159f7452d258ea1f437cbf4f42d1d01ed1f) (see diagram) produces the standard equation of the ellipse:[[3]](#cite_note-mathworld-3)
![{\displaystyle {\frac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5e04cef1c6af3e391a7fe772f38ce56bd0a71cc5)
or, solved for *y*:
![{\displaystyle y=\pm {\frac {b}{a}}{\sqrt {{\vphantom {\left(a^{2}-x^{2}\right)}}a^{2}-x^{2}}}=\pm {\sqrt {\left(a^{2}-x^{2}\right)\left(1-e^{2}\right)}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e873cf84681edb31f96abffcfa19fad1b87315b6)

The width and height parameters ![{\displaystyle a,\;b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1c747ea5fdf635321385220fd12675e9c1257a02) are called the [semi-major and semi-minor axes](/wiki/Semi-major_and_semi-minor_axes "Semi-major and semi-minor axes"). The top and bottom points ![{\displaystyle V_{3}=(0,\,b),\;V_{4}=(0,\,-b)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0361b8a414692de64787a884718974a874b61205) are the *co-vertices*. The distances from a point ![{\displaystyle (x,\,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0e7afe5870de593e694568d1c4b0b95c6ac50777) on the ellipse to the left and right foci are ![{\displaystyle a+ex}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ea24691d9d5d8121b75e22bda982f2f2d626779c) and ![{\displaystyle a-ex}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2a30330cfabfb68cdb6376693f33959ec1c09026).

It follows from the equation that the ellipse is *symmetric* with respect to the coordinate axes and hence with respect to the origin.

### Parameters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Parameters")]

#### Principal axes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Principal axes")]

Throughout this article, the [semi-major and semi-minor axes](/wiki/Semi-major_and_semi-minor_axes "Semi-major and semi-minor axes") are denoted ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) and ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3), respectively, i.e. ![{\displaystyle a\geq b>0\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c6a707a53ecd77a2ad7084d41980ec4b9e9f65e)

In principle, the canonical ellipse equation ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f6aa55c90da259c96a7894fe6e7555d2428cdec3) may have ![{\displaystyle a<b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/91a7698e4c7401bb321f97888b872b583a9e4642) (and hence the ellipse would be taller than it is wide). This form can be converted to the standard form by transposing the variable names ![{\displaystyle x}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/87f9e315fd7e2ba406057a97300593c4802b53e4) and ![{\displaystyle y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b8a6208ec717213d4317e666f1ae872e00620a0d) and the parameter names ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) and ![{\displaystyle b.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/aef051eb30c89e5493d672f6479566c673b0890a)

#### Linear eccentricity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Linear eccentricity")]

This is the distance from the center to a focus: ![{\displaystyle c={\sqrt {a^{2}-b^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/78a9d2e194c630274028a18c59e4289981aad14d).

#### Eccentricity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Eccentricity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Pythagorean_theorem_ellipse_eccentricity.svg/250px-Pythagorean_theorem_ellipse_eccentricity.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pythagorean_theorem_ellipse_eccentricity.svg)

Eccentricity *e* in terms of semi-major *a* and semi-minor *b* axes: *e*² + (*b/a*)² = 1

The eccentricity can be expressed as:
![{\displaystyle e={\frac {c}{a}}={\sqrt {1-\left({\frac {b}{a}}\right)^{2}}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fb6578ede42b375945ba31efccda708a8571cf24)

assuming ![{\displaystyle a>b.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/19f3dbc209b9e7a3f89d6b918f0f67a5fd7cc2d6) An ellipse with equal axes (![{\displaystyle a=b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1956b03d1314c7071ac1f45ed7b1e29422dcfcc4)) has zero eccentricity, and is a circle.

#### Semi-latus rectum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Semi-latus rectum")]

The length of the chord through one focus, perpendicular to the major axis, is called the *latus rectum*. One half of it is the *semi-latus rectum* ![{\displaystyle \ell }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f066e981e530bacc07efc6a10fa82deee985929e). A calculation shows:[[4]](#cite_note-4)
![{\displaystyle \ell ={\frac {b^{2}}{a}}=a\left(1-e^{2}\right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d1b231abf7286be8d953bed52267aad458875db5)

The semi-latus rectum ![{\displaystyle \ell }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f066e981e530bacc07efc6a10fa82deee985929e) is equal to the [radius of curvature](/wiki/Radius_of_curvature "Radius of curvature") at the vertices (see section [curvature](#Curvature)).

### Tangent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Tangent")]

An arbitrary line ![{\displaystyle g}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3556280e66fe2c0d0140df20935a6f057381d77) intersects an ellipse at 0, 1, or 2 points, respectively called an *exterior line*, *tangent* and *secant*. Through any point of an ellipse there is a unique tangent. The tangent at a point ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},\,y_{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c9e693bb3fab45df3151c136a55b3419ed0f799b) of the ellipse ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f6aa55c90da259c96a7894fe6e7555d2428cdec3) has the coordinate equation:
![{\displaystyle {\frac {x_{1}}{a^{2}}}x+{\frac {y_{1}}{b^{2}}}y=1.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/994f2b6b5927b4db93c30b28c25a1d6003952169)

A vector [parametric equation](/wiki/Parametric_equation "Parametric equation") of the tangent is:
![{\displaystyle {\vec {x}}={\begin{pmatrix}x_{1}\\y_{1}\end{pmatrix}}+s\left({\begin{array}{r}-y_{1}a^{2}\\x_{1}b^{2}\end{array}}\right),\quad s\in \mathbb {R} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e2c37da74ce0608c17b2a97e6c0b2acc1678fdda)

**Proof:**
Let ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},\,y_{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c9e693bb3fab45df3151c136a55b3419ed0f799b) be a point on an ellipse and ![{\textstyle {\vec {x}}={\begin{pmatrix}x_{1}\\y_{1}\end{pmatrix}}+s{\begin{pmatrix}u\\v\end{pmatrix}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/502a140a5d58126eafda333729f3f8087bbcd72a) be the equation of any line ![{\displaystyle g}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3556280e66fe2c0d0140df20935a6f057381d77) containing ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},\,y_{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c9e693bb3fab45df3151c136a55b3419ed0f799b). Inserting the line's equation into the ellipse equation and respecting ![{\textstyle {\frac {x_{1}^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {y_{1}^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d5af6060ad79e43e38eba5c8c2f3b0d74273b8f5) yields:
![{\displaystyle {\frac {\left(x_{1}+su\right)^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {\left(y_{1}+sv\right)^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1\ \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2s\left({\frac {x_{1}u}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {y_{1}v}{b^{2}}}\right)+s^{2}\left({\frac {u^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {v^{2}}{b^{2}}}\right)=0\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8a1f5318d9c1ee7102a56ece447258162a149b42)
There are then cases:

1. ![{\displaystyle {\frac {x_{1}}{a^{2}}}u+{\frac {y_{1}}{b^{2}}}v=0.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dd90222636919d3b3bb29f503789629aba0a84e6) Then line ![{\displaystyle g}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3556280e66fe2c0d0140df20935a6f057381d77) and the ellipse have only point ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},\,y_{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c9e693bb3fab45df3151c136a55b3419ed0f799b) in common, and ![{\displaystyle g}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3556280e66fe2c0d0140df20935a6f057381d77) is a tangent. The tangent direction has [perpendicular vector](/wiki/Normal_(geometry) "Normal (geometry)") ![{\displaystyle {\begin{pmatrix}{\frac {x_{1}}{a^{2}}}&{\frac {y_{1}}{b^{2}}}\end{pmatrix}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2998c465c3d326d8c90db3f2eb61ed357fd6a868), so the tangent line has equation ![{\textstyle {\frac {x_{1}}{a^{2}}}x+{\tfrac {y_{1}}{b^{2}}}y=k}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ad1109488d3605c807c39dc1f806ffca4becb3ae) for some ![{\displaystyle k}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c3c9a2c7b599b37105512c5d570edc034056dd40). Because ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},\,y_{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c9e693bb3fab45df3151c136a55b3419ed0f799b) is on the tangent and the ellipse, one obtains ![{\displaystyle k=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6c035ffa69b5bca8bf2d16c3da3aaad79a8bcbfa).
2. ![{\displaystyle {\frac {x_{1}}{a^{2}}}u+{\frac {y_{1}}{b^{2}}}v\neq 0.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6a3478011aa8d8687991a21c79f579cdc4e037d6) Then line ![{\displaystyle g}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3556280e66fe2c0d0140df20935a6f057381d77) has a second point in common with the ellipse, and is a secant.

Using (1) one finds that ![{\displaystyle {\begin{pmatrix}-y_{1}a^{2}&x_{1}b^{2}\end{pmatrix}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7820c99cbf4021be7a0fd4117c8e01d5ad6f6dae) is a tangent vector at point ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},\,y_{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c9e693bb3fab45df3151c136a55b3419ed0f799b), which proves the vector equation.

If ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},y_{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7fc74086e56542bd28b46a84faaee3cebdd4a899) and ![{\displaystyle (u,v)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eadf12294edccd7a29c99cfc1765e4a14bf47e58) are two points of the ellipse such that ![{\textstyle {\frac {x_{1}u}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y_{1}v}{b^{2}}}=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1a7f64600f5647ec96e4cc969472704a74577f04), then the points lie on two *conjugate diameters* (see [below](#Conjugate_diameters)). (If ![{\displaystyle a=b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1956b03d1314c7071ac1f45ed7b1e29422dcfcc4), the ellipse is a circle and "conjugate" means "orthogonal".)

### Shifted ellipse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Shifted ellipse")]

If the standard ellipse is shifted to have center ![{\displaystyle \left(x_{\circ },\,y_{\circ }\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/594dd3c3be61b6829e3415a0c988565fd529e6b9), its equation is
![{\displaystyle {\frac {\left(x-x_{\circ }\right)^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {\left(y-y_{\circ }\right)^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fe592d041402e4b3e54a69b154f497af44fccda8)

The axes are still parallel to the *x*- and *y*-axes.

### General ellipse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: General ellipse")]

Main article: [Matrix representation of conic sections](/wiki/Matrix_representation_of_conic_sections "Matrix representation of conic sections")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/General_ellipse.png/330px-General_ellipse.png)](/wiki/File:General_ellipse.png)

A general ellipse in the plane can be uniquely described as a bivariate quadratic equation of Cartesian coordinates, or using center, semi-major and semi-minor axes, and angle

In [analytic geometry](/wiki/Analytic_geometry "Analytic geometry"), the ellipse is defined as a [quadric](/wiki/Quadratic_form "Quadratic form"): the set of points ![{\displaystyle (x,\,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0e7afe5870de593e694568d1c4b0b95c6ac50777) of the [Cartesian plane](/wiki/Cartesian_plane "Cartesian plane") that, in non-degenerate cases, satisfy the [implicit](/wiki/Implicit_and_explicit_functions "Implicit and explicit functions") equation[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)
![{\displaystyle Ax^{2}+Bxy+Cy^{2}+Dx+Ey+F=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a694f3a0805f2f11868a2a69413866d64170409b)
provided ![{\displaystyle B^{2}-4AC<0.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4f9b3184559e82578c79d0efc54045fd4982c5d5)

To distinguish the [degenerate cases](/wiki/Degenerate_conic "Degenerate conic") from the non-degenerate case, let *∆* be the [determinant](/wiki/Determinant "Determinant")
![{\displaystyle \Delta ={\begin{vmatrix}A&{\frac {1}{2}}B&{\frac {1}{2}}D\\{\frac {1}{2}}B&C&{\frac {1}{2}}E\\{\frac {1}{2}}D&{\frac {1}{2}}E&F\end{vmatrix}}=ACF+{\tfrac {1}{4}}BDE-{\tfrac {1}{4}}(AE^{2}+CD^{2}+FB^{2}).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c7667aecb971fe0f450b0ff40a0196f7fe729974)

Then the ellipse is a non-degenerate real ellipse if and only if *C∆* < 0. If *C∆* > 0, we have an imaginary ellipse, and if *∆* = 0, we have a point ellipse.[[7]](#cite_note-Lawrence-7): 63

The general equation's coefficients can be obtained from known semi-major axis ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc), semi-minor axis ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3), center coordinates ![{\displaystyle \left(x_{\circ },\,y_{\circ }\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/594dd3c3be61b6829e3415a0c988565fd529e6b9), and rotation angle ![{\displaystyle \theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e5ab2664b422d53eb0c7df3b87e1360d75ad9af) (the angle from the positive horizontal axis to the ellipse's major axis) using the formulae:
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}A&=a^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta +b^{2}\cos ^{2}\theta &B&=2\left(b^{2}-a^{2}\right)\sin \theta \cos \theta \\[1ex]C&=a^{2}\cos ^{2}\theta +b^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta &D&=-2Ax_{\circ }-By_{\circ }\\[1ex]E&=-Bx_{\circ }-2Cy_{\circ }&F&=Ax_{\circ }^{2}+Bx_{\circ }y_{\circ }+Cy_{\circ }^{2}-a^{2}b^{2}.\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/83709b15a759e09332f42231c992ef5f97e6e2cb)

These expressions can be derived from the canonical equation
![{\displaystyle {\frac {X^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {Y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d4893cd8abdbbe27d217784fcb73a7c7047c2736)
by a Euclidean transformation of the coordinates ![{\displaystyle (X,\,Y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5f67132f4114622e831c92844f14318c05cb1213):
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}X&=\left(x-x_{\circ }\right)\cos \theta +\left(y-y_{\circ }\right)\sin \theta ,\\Y&=-\left(x-x_{\circ }\right)\sin \theta +\left(y-y_{\circ }\right)\cos \theta .\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fee05ae05b0c85ac26e036e1ce7151ba0907e6ea)

Conversely, the canonical form parameters can be obtained from the general-form coefficients by the equations:[[3]](#cite_note-mathworld-3)

![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}a,b&={\frac {-{\sqrt {2{\big (}AE^{2}+CD^{2}-BDE+(B^{2}-4AC)F{\big )}{\big (}(A+C)\pm {\sqrt {(A-C)^{2}+B^{2}}}{\big )}}}}{B^{2}-4AC}},\\x_{\circ }&={\frac {2CD-BE}{B^{2}-4AC}},\\[5mu]y_{\circ }&={\frac {2AE-BD}{B^{2}-4AC}},\\[5mu]\theta &={\tfrac {1}{2}}\operatorname {atan2} (-B,\,C-A),\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/49c699db33888167e2c0c9fb5223b3b11d5124f9)

where [atan2](/wiki/Atan2 "Atan2") is the 2-argument arctangent function.

## Parametric representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Parametric representation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Elliko-sk.svg/250px-Elliko-sk.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Elliko-sk.svg)

The construction of points based on the parametric equation and the interpretation of parameter *t*, which is due to de la Hire

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Ellipse-ratpar.svg/250px-Ellipse-ratpar.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-ratpar.svg)

Ellipse points calculated by the rational representation with equally spaced parameters (![{\displaystyle \Delta u=0.2}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5e4af5ab76e5e90c449a38a7bdf68ed82acae499)).

### Standard parametric representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Standard parametric representation")]

Using [trigonometric functions](/wiki/Trigonometric_function "Trigonometric function"), a parametric representation of the standard ellipse ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f6aa55c90da259c96a7894fe6e7555d2428cdec3) is:
![{\displaystyle (x,\,y)=(a\cos t,\,b\sin t),\ 0\leq t<2\pi \,.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c6a8b6e45332f2527bada46aa09e100ef26c23de)

The parameter *t* (called the *[eccentric anomaly](/wiki/Eccentric_anomaly "Eccentric anomaly")* in astronomy) is not the angle of ![{\displaystyle (x(t),y(t))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b7b80868600db89fef84e4d41317b7c8a1e0d047) with the *x*-axis, but has a geometric meaning due to [Philippe de La Hire](/wiki/Philippe_de_La_Hire "Philippe de La Hire") (see *[§ Drawing ellipses](#Drawing_ellipses)* below).[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Rational representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Rational representation")]

With the substitution ![{\textstyle u=\tan \left({\frac {t}{2}}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9cc670f0f67b5cab3b4f2c132355cd4d55d04e2b) and trigonometric formulae one obtains
![{\displaystyle \cos t={\frac {1-u^{2}}{1+u^{2}}}\ ,\quad \sin t={\frac {2u}{1+u^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5a7189fb575455b221bb999b1b5d5f12ba8fe5dd)

and the *rational* parametric equation of an ellipse
![{\displaystyle {\begin{cases}x(u)=a\,{\dfrac {1-u^{2}}{1+u^{2}}}\\[10mu]y(u)=b\,{\dfrac {2u}{1+u^{2}}}\\[10mu]-\infty <u<\infty \end{cases}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/338f80cb5063c4db7bb394618c0012575c6ae071)

which covers any point of the ellipse ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f6aa55c90da259c96a7894fe6e7555d2428cdec3) except the left vertex ![{\displaystyle (-a,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4fe512bfe9855846883a7e88318129516cc12151).

For ![{\displaystyle u\in [0,\,1],}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c61b780db9ac550dd283876e16abe9c2cccdf8c3) this formula represents the right upper quarter of the ellipse moving counter-clockwise with increasing ![{\displaystyle u.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/edd5636410da69bac33da075162221527401793c) The left vertex is the limit ![{\textstyle \lim _{u\to \pm \infty }(x(u),\,y(u))=(-a,\,0)\;.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/54df8d27bbc9cbe106710f10ee0f715961c7fbaf)

Alternately, if the parameter ![{\displaystyle [u:v]}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/297de5a93c52f13ef84add1d79d693fcda686176) is considered to be a point on the [real projective line](/wiki/Real_projective_line "Real projective line") ![{\textstyle \mathbf {P} (\mathbf {R} )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/09fcebc435e5694c8c37f590332fae766bd4aa32), then the corresponding rational parametrization is
![{\displaystyle [u:v]\mapsto \left(a{\frac {v^{2}-u^{2}}{v^{2}+u^{2}}},b{\frac {2uv}{v^{2}+u^{2}}}\right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fa1c21205522d70966ce62b8e324b19ec4c90f41)

Then ![{\textstyle [1:0]\mapsto (-a,\,0).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0da12e69b8138a30dcb6cfbeeb95bd63b890f2db)

Rational representations of conic sections are commonly used in [computer-aided design](/wiki/Computer-aided_design "Computer-aided design") (see [Bézier curve](/wiki/B%C3%A9zier_curve#Rational_Bézier_curves "Bézier curve")).

### Tangent slope as parameter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Tangent slope as parameter")]

A parametric representation, which uses the slope ![{\displaystyle m}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0a07d98bb302f3856cbabc47b2b9016692e3f7bc) of the tangent at a point of the ellipse
can be obtained from the derivative of the standard representation ![{\displaystyle {\vec {x}}(t)=(a\cos t,\,b\sin t)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5db22818fffc6202d17608b1b75b23c3b58a073):
![{\displaystyle {\vec {x}}'(t)=(-a\sin t,\,b\cos t)\quad \rightarrow \quad m=-{\frac {b}{a}}\cot t\quad \rightarrow \quad \cot t=-{\frac {ma}{b}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4bea8b3487773b968b873b5ac9892efe1005d42b)

With help of [trigonometric formulae](/wiki/List_of_trigonometric_identities#Pythagorean_identities "List of trigonometric identities") one obtains:
![{\displaystyle \cos t={\frac {\cot t}{\pm {\sqrt {1+\cot ^{2}t}}}}={\frac {-ma}{\pm {\sqrt {m^{2}a^{2}+b^{2}}}}}\ ,\quad \quad \sin t={\frac {1}{\pm {\sqrt {1+\cot ^{2}t}}}}={\frac {b}{\pm {\sqrt {m^{2}a^{2}+b^{2}}}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ee220fb861e4f2af754be48014bf7d14fe43c08f)

Replacing ![{\displaystyle \cos t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3798aceb2c56c130331f6fc331815306df597de5) and ![{\displaystyle \sin t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2b812327f691449768f64b4fe8b27c8468ec1ad8) of the standard representation yields:
![{\displaystyle {\vec {c}}_{\pm }(m)=\left(-{\frac {ma^{2}}{\pm {\sqrt {m^{2}a^{2}+b^{2}}}}},\;{\frac {b^{2}}{\pm {\sqrt {m^{2}a^{2}+b^{2}}}}}\right),\,m\in \mathbb {R} .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8dbfb90854a74813eaa3c976a52449276ec0969e)

Here ![{\displaystyle m}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0a07d98bb302f3856cbabc47b2b9016692e3f7bc) is the slope of the tangent at the corresponding ellipse point, ![{\displaystyle {\vec {c}}_{+}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2b85590e6ed1728a79fdf8dae477b8f013612a4e) is the upper and ![{\displaystyle {\vec {c}}_{-}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c5b65dad8e65b6cd632157ffed5f6aceb0f6194) the lower half of the ellipse. The vertices![{\displaystyle (\pm a,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7e8e23ad7d9cd0e871b043470943354c22b4a2fd), having vertical tangents, are not covered by the representation.

The equation of the tangent at point ![{\displaystyle {\vec {c}}_{\pm }(m)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2a28b30737d3f9305e24285710f2ee477f51328c) has the form ![{\displaystyle y=mx+n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0ae774e96bc6f9f2eb75c360415fe3096dd3e8e1). The still unknown ![{\displaystyle n}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a601995d55609f2d9f5e233e36fbe9ea26011b3b) can be determined by inserting the coordinates of the corresponding ellipse point ![{\displaystyle {\vec {c}}_{\pm }(m)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2a28b30737d3f9305e24285710f2ee477f51328c):
![{\displaystyle y=mx\pm {\sqrt {m^{2}a^{2}+b^{2}}}\,.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eb7fb21427332172d11a63969d63344d0da5b314)

This description of the tangents of an ellipse is an essential tool for the determination of the [orthoptic](/wiki/Orthoptic_(geometry) "Orthoptic (geometry)") of an ellipse. The orthoptic article contains another proof, without differential calculus and trigonometric formulae.

### General ellipse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: General ellipse")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Ellipse-aff.svg/250px-Ellipse-aff.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-aff.svg)

Ellipse as an affine image of the unit circle

Another definition of an ellipse uses [affine transformations](/wiki/Affine_transformation "Affine transformation"):

:   Any *ellipse* is an affine image of the unit circle with equation ![{\displaystyle x^{2}+y^{2}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec84b90236512e8d27ff1a8f7707b60b63327de1).

Parametric representation

An affine transformation of the Euclidean plane has the form ![{\displaystyle {\vec {x}}\mapsto {\vec {f}}\!_{0}+A{\vec {x}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/765a6a8086cb3c5513f93e196e9e9bde509ca130), where ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3) is a regular [matrix](/wiki/Matrix_(mathematics) "Matrix (mathematics)") (with non-zero [determinant](/wiki/Determinant "Determinant")) and ![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}\!_{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/775a1987137c53250767ed16f0b3cca2be1a40b1) is an arbitrary vector. If ![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}\!_{1},{\vec {f}}\!_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5349e23f6b09d36c66fffca971ffcb7ab01c54b0) are the column vectors of the matrix ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3), the unit circle ![{\displaystyle (\cos(t),\sin(t))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/825ae60ea44061a054246b0642ba2932d3acc805), ![{\displaystyle 0\leq t\leq 2\pi }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2bc01113133d5e7f6342e1a4d738e0d84e57b54d), is mapped onto the ellipse:
![{\displaystyle {\vec {x}}={\vec {p}}(t)={\vec {f}}\!_{0}+{\vec {f}}\!_{1}\cos t+{\vec {f}}\!_{2}\sin t\,.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fef85173da638d1800ef1867006b7d4178bbeaf3)

Here ![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}\!_{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/775a1987137c53250767ed16f0b3cca2be1a40b1) is the center and ![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}\!_{1},\;{\vec {f}}\!_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/071aca443a87e9c70d40e17bd5fe258381ffda2c) are the directions of two [conjugate diameters](/wiki/Conjugate_diameter "Conjugate diameter"), in general not perpendicular.

Vertices

The four vertices of the ellipse are ![{\displaystyle {\vec {p}}(t_{0}),\;{\vec {p}}\left(t_{0}\pm {\tfrac {\pi }{2}}\right),\;{\vec {p}}\left(t_{0}+\pi \right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f04f6c331d674ab734b11b2aaa2a4fd29169bb21), for a parameter ![{\displaystyle t=t_{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/be6d7492e2d48bf34fdd5dffa189b188c140820c) defined by:
![{\displaystyle \cot(2t_{0})={\frac {{\vec {f}}\!_{1}^{\,2}-{\vec {f}}\!_{2}^{\,2}}{2{\vec {f}}\!_{1}\cdot {\vec {f}}\!_{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7c2a024ba383d1cac80743170647422bfb87685c)

(If ![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}\!_{1}\cdot {\vec {f}}\!_{2}=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/39f6142b147dbafab84999ed36cf021906e85d3b), then ![{\displaystyle t_{0}=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/129dee73708133aabd89cd4adf5f38ebcaf23a86).) This is derived as follows. The tangent vector at point ![{\displaystyle {\vec {p}}(t)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/676ccbdf4a55f2d8a1ef2608679a465cd7172733) is:
![{\displaystyle {\vec {p}}\,'(t)=-{\vec {f}}\!_{1}\sin t+{\vec {f}}\!_{2}\cos t\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/950660252c671af9322fa34cd0dcba168dffb844)

At a vertex parameter ![{\displaystyle t=t_{0}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/be6d7492e2d48bf34fdd5dffa189b188c140820c), the tangent is perpendicular to the major/minor axes, so:
![{\displaystyle 0={\vec {p}}'(t)\cdot \left({\vec {p}}(t)-{\vec {f}}\!_{0}\right)=\left(-{\vec {f}}\!_{1}\sin t+{\vec {f}}\!_{2}\cos t\right)\cdot \left({\vec {f}}\!_{1}\cos t+{\vec {f}}\!_{2}\sin t\right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b5317c56e0f6340051852578b74af339d21f93a8)

Expanding and applying the identities ![{\displaystyle \;\cos ^{2}t-\sin ^{2}t=\cos 2t,\ \ 2\sin t\cos t=\sin 2t\;}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6ccfc91fb92b3e2bfb02afb84a1180919871f338) gives the equation for ![{\displaystyle t=t_{0}\;.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/721cd6f95dd38f79374f35186f20fe6739cf1887)

Area

From Apollonios theorem (see below) one obtains:  
The area of an ellipse ![{\displaystyle \;{\vec {x}}={\vec {f}}_{0}+{\vec {f}}_{1}\cos t+{\vec {f}}_{2}\sin t\;}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c0e761a7b96a311212a60d6d0e48bb1eac1656f4) is
![{\displaystyle A=\pi \left|\det({\vec {f}}_{1},{\vec {f}}_{2})\right|.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6ff0a1cfcb84368d61fd6751bb0a0061a5ec72a4)

Semiaxes

With the abbreviations
![{\displaystyle \;M={\vec {f}}_{1}^{2}+{\vec {f}}_{2}^{2},\ N=\left|\det({\vec {f}}_{1},{\vec {f}}_{2})\right|}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7f9c92f0e6c08494d0e1b5c3f21d3ca4961e7556) the statements of Apollonios's theorem can be written as:
![{\displaystyle a^{2}+b^{2}=M,\quad ab=N\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/75a10a2d43fd8b7ed512d8f6a3f7639e419340ed)
Solving this [nonlinear system](/wiki/Nonlinear_system "Nonlinear system") for ![{\displaystyle a,b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/181523deba732fda302fd176275a0739121d3bc8) yields the semiaxes:
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}a&={\frac {1}{2}}({\sqrt {M+2N}}+{\sqrt {M-2N}})\\[1ex]b&={\frac {1}{2}}({\sqrt {M+2N}}-{\sqrt {M-2N}})\,.\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8252bcba269bad4896aef5f664a83860320d49b5)

Implicit representation

Solving the parametric representation for ![{\displaystyle \;\cos t,\sin t\;}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/767d719746b2bee4ce475d558e119b8f148d6417) by [Cramer's rule](/wiki/Cramer%27s_rule "Cramer's rule") and using ![{\displaystyle \;\cos ^{2}t+\sin ^{2}t-1=0\;}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/272bd71be66bea78c629820ca1dd61517066b4f6), one obtains the implicit representation
![{\displaystyle \det {\left({\vec {x}}\!-\!{\vec {f}}\!_{0},{\vec {f}}\!_{2}\right)^{2}}+\det {\left({\vec {f}}\!_{1},{\vec {x}}\!-\!{\vec {f}}\!_{0}\right)^{2}}-\det {\left({\vec {f}}\!_{1},{\vec {f}}\!_{2}\right)^{2}}=0.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6564e4e06c01a21403f09519df396028cb475fc4)

Conversely: If the [equation](/wiki/Matrix_representation_of_conic_sections "Matrix representation of conic sections")

:   ![{\displaystyle x^{2}+2cxy+d^{2}y^{2}-e^{2}=0\ ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8dc23689c7a60a4a635a525704188427e699452d) with ![{\displaystyle \;d^{2}-c^{2}>0\;,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f8ad11ee9f6ae2583717cc6b02ba0a4928d8e90d)

of an ellipse centered at the origin is given, then the two vectors
![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}_{1}={e \choose 0},\quad {\vec {f}}_{2}={\frac {e}{\sqrt {d^{2}-c^{2}}}}{-c \choose 1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/30025bec07094fb2738c1468c8eff48306c745e4)
point to two conjugate points and the tools developed above are applicable.

*Example*: For the ellipse with equation ![{\displaystyle \;x^{2}+2xy+3y^{2}-1=0\;}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6db46cd2bb525de1263e59c13402b486394d1588) the vectors are
![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}_{1}={1 \choose 0},\quad {\vec {f}}_{2}={\frac {1}{\sqrt {2}}}{-1 \choose 1}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/07013b531022b3b9b250ca8a5ad2f17ab46d970e)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Nested_Ellipses.svg/330px-Nested_Ellipses.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nested_Ellipses.svg)

Whirls: nested, scaled and rotated ellipses. The spiral is not drawn: we see it as the [locus](/wiki/Locus_(mathematics) "Locus (mathematics)") of points where the ellipses are especially close to each other.

Rotated standard ellipse

For ![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}_{0}={0 \choose 0},\;{\vec {f}}_{1}=a{\cos \theta  \choose \sin \theta },\;{\vec {f}}_{2}=b{-\sin \theta  \choose \;\cos \theta }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2112be8a1a759f7dc09d909a1ba38bf706cc3957) one obtains a parametric representation of the standard ellipse [rotated](/wiki/Rotation_matrix "Rotation matrix") by angle ![{\displaystyle \theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e5ab2664b422d53eb0c7df3b87e1360d75ad9af):
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}x&=x_{\theta }(t)=a\cos \theta \cos t-b\sin \theta \sin t\,,\\y&=y_{\theta }(t)=a\sin \theta \cos t+b\cos \theta \sin t\,.\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4a1061a5327c5b254fbf8f1c404c0056298f3c66)

Ellipse in space

The definition of an ellipse in this section gives a parametric representation of an arbitrary ellipse, even in space, if one allows ![{\displaystyle {\vec {f}}\!_{0},{\vec {f}}\!_{1},{\vec {f}}\!_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a2937be993ed4d9a5b777ff4b4e7274c256e7f83) to be vectors in space.

## Polar forms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Polar forms")]

### Polar form relative to center

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Polar form relative to center")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Ellipse_Polar_center.svg/250px-Ellipse_Polar_center.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse_Polar_center.svg)

Polar coordinates centered at the center.

In [polar coordinates](/wiki/Polar_coordinates "Polar coordinates"), with the origin at the center of the ellipse and with the angular coordinate ![{\displaystyle \theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e5ab2664b422d53eb0c7df3b87e1360d75ad9af) measured from the major axis, the ellipse's equation is[[7]](#cite_note-Lawrence-7): 75
![{\displaystyle r(\theta )={\frac {ab}{\sqrt {(b\cos \theta )^{2}+(a\sin \theta )^{2}}}}={\frac {b}{\sqrt {1-(e\cos \theta )^{2}}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/703b7f26bb0a4a8f49a499d6d56b5e2cfbd407a7)
where ![{\displaystyle e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd253103f0876afc68ebead27a5aa9867d927467) is the eccentricity (not [Euler's number](/wiki/Euler%27s_number "Euler's number")).

### Polar form relative to focus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Polar form relative to focus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Ellipse_Polar.svg/250px-Ellipse_Polar.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse_Polar.svg)

Polar coordinates centered at focus.

If instead we use polar coordinates with the origin at one focus, with the angular coordinate ![{\displaystyle \theta =0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2a7bc6e34b53e0e8a8815159c356b1acccf7ea24) still measured from the major axis, the ellipse's equation is
![{\displaystyle r(\theta )={\frac {a(1-e^{2})}{1\pm e\cos \theta }}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ce854b0567e04582334d963cce256d81717693bd)

where the sign in the denominator is negative if the reference direction ![{\displaystyle \theta =0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2a7bc6e34b53e0e8a8815159c356b1acccf7ea24) points towards the center (as illustrated on the right), and positive if that direction points away from the center.

The angle ![{\displaystyle \theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6e5ab2664b422d53eb0c7df3b87e1360d75ad9af) is called the [true anomaly](/wiki/True_anomaly "True anomaly") of the point. The numerator ![{\displaystyle \ell =a(1-e^{2})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f35706c7dc28606b92ae708031381a66623d5f1a) is the [semi-latus rectum](/wiki/Semi-latus_rectum "Semi-latus rectum").

## Eccentricity and the directrix property

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Eccentricity and the directrix property")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Ellipse-ll-e.svg/250px-Ellipse-ll-e.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-ll-e.svg)

Ellipse: directrix property

Each of the two lines parallel to the minor axis, and at a distance of ![{\textstyle d={\frac {a^{2}}{c}}={\frac {a}{e}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/db40f2f0dd19d95c7e1d640caf63e1e135698773) from it, is called a *directrix* of the ellipse (see diagram).

:   For an arbitrary point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) of the ellipse, the quotient of the distance to one focus and to the corresponding directrix (see diagram) is equal to the eccentricity: ![{\displaystyle {\frac {\left|PF_{1}\right|}{\left|Pl_{1}\right|}}={\frac {\left|PF_{2}\right|}{\left|Pl_{2}\right|}}=e={\frac {c}{a}}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f4d37d64e2187264b82540a41b4eb2ff7fa21c15)

The proof for the pair ![{\displaystyle F_{1},l_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/13366c3fb9a07c61e03716c2fc53387a1d391626) follows from the fact that ![{\textstyle \left|PF_{1}\right|^{2}=(x-c)^{2}+y^{2},\ \left|Pl_{1}\right|^{2}=\left(x-{\tfrac {a^{2}}{c}}\right)^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1aac284e537d1400031bda094a0b9d59294fa656) and ![{\displaystyle y^{2}=b^{2}-{\tfrac {b^{2}}{a^{2}}}x^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2afecde9655b414988209c37cc31993dc47ff797) satisfy the equation
![{\displaystyle \left|PF_{1}\right|^{2}-{\frac {c^{2}}{a^{2}}}\left|Pl_{1}\right|^{2}=0\,.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3ba7ed8d12c2904e22e0b35188212a1142988406)

The second case is proven analogously.

The converse is also true and can be used to define an ellipse (in a manner similar to the definition of a parabola):

:   For any point ![{\displaystyle F}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/545fd099af8541605f7ee55f08225526be88ce57) (focus), any line ![{\displaystyle l}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/829091f745070b9eb97a80244129025440a1cfac) (directrix) not through ![{\displaystyle F}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/545fd099af8541605f7ee55f08225526be88ce57), and any [real number](/wiki/Real_number "Real number") ![{\displaystyle e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd253103f0876afc68ebead27a5aa9867d927467) with ![{\displaystyle 0<e<1,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/492ac9a910c6a74f38c4d19cafde2f25509caeb2) the ellipse is the locus of points for which the quotient of the distances to the point and to the line is ![{\displaystyle e,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1cfc3e7aeae6ef256412eaa9f68d097804943b5a) that is: ![{\displaystyle E=\left\{P\ \left|\ {\frac {|PF|}{|Pl|}}=e\right.\right\}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/abb7bfa9a0dbb4b0faade9e4e6618b4996c11ac2)

The extension to ![{\displaystyle e=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d9850169d70a5ab7df71c2126441a86cec93eec8), which is the eccentricity of a circle, is not allowed in this context in the Euclidean plane. However, one may consider the directrix of a circle to be the [line at infinity](/wiki/Line_at_infinity "Line at infinity") in the [projective plane](/wiki/Projective_plane "Projective plane").

(The choice ![{\displaystyle e=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c2f5932668126c63c844dc00ca187bc58a29e5a) yields a parabola, and if ![{\displaystyle e>1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9605ca17e3915b659685c0326fbbcbfb522f11b3), a hyperbola.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Kegelschnitt-schar-ev.svg/250px-Kegelschnitt-schar-ev.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kegelschnitt-schar-ev.svg)

Pencil of conics with a common vertex and common semi-latus rectum

Proof

Let ![{\displaystyle F=(f,\,0),\ e>0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bfd147cff6705625f8bba0e2b98233f80546551f), and assume ![{\displaystyle (0,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/88ab8060ed06b3b7a806ded6d570097fbabb1eff) is a point on the curve. The directrix ![{\displaystyle l}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/829091f745070b9eb97a80244129025440a1cfac) has equation ![{\displaystyle x=-{\tfrac {f}{e}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5efeb568559a2dead06289ba0040decd175349e4). With ![{\displaystyle P=(x,\,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3c1bacb7389aa017236ab2ff91a2349858f13709), the relation ![{\displaystyle |PF|^{2}=e^{2}|Pl|^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4cb3d29844176603758b959f6aabc860df7369cd) produces the equations

:   ![{\displaystyle (x-f)^{2}+y^{2}=e^{2}\left(x+{\frac {f}{e}}\right)^{2}=(ex+f)^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8c15d82159ffa00a97f1d133e8058df293bab415) and ![{\displaystyle x^{2}\left(e^{2}-1\right)+2xf(1+e)-y^{2}=0.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c41e0cb2a3ec5fb24440b316b2a9c4308b887584)

The substitution ![{\displaystyle p=f(1+e)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c20f2c9e9a54aed3dad1b35cbccf501213fb2953) yields
![{\displaystyle x^{2}\left(e^{2}-1\right)+2px-y^{2}=0.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/59da332c87bf913b09739dfd230a5f0564d00798)

This is the equation of an *ellipse* (![{\displaystyle e<1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2c702b141b56e60fdcb2c72832b2dcf0484607af)), or a *parabola* (![{\displaystyle e=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c2f5932668126c63c844dc00ca187bc58a29e5a)), or a *hyperbola* (![{\displaystyle e>1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9605ca17e3915b659685c0326fbbcbfb522f11b3)). All of these non-degenerate conics have, in common, the origin as a vertex (see diagram).

If ![{\displaystyle e<1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2c702b141b56e60fdcb2c72832b2dcf0484607af), introduce new parameters ![{\displaystyle a,\,b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c12ceaff955e1a98e48db7fa8fa43121630fa209) so that ![{\displaystyle 1-e^{2}={\tfrac {b^{2}}{a^{2}}},{\text{ and }}\ p={\tfrac {b^{2}}{a}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/eb9bb742cc1c435b0e2963eb28da567f1f0865d7), and then the equation above becomes
![{\displaystyle {\frac {(x-a)^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1\,,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/07fc0d8862dc56cc65770ff4d4ade2c516e7cd94)

which is the equation of an ellipse with center ![{\displaystyle (a,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7fcb754671c31a951bc2d59bef7ce46e4ba3ed1e), the *x*-axis as major axis, and
the major/minor semi axis ![{\displaystyle a,\,b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c12ceaff955e1a98e48db7fa8fa43121630fa209).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Leitlinien-konstr-e.svg/250px-Leitlinien-konstr-e.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Leitlinien-konstr-e.svg)

Construction of a directrix

Construction of a directrix

Because of ![{\displaystyle c\cdot {\tfrac {a^{2}}{c}}=a^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6dcec6bf79073d464792338044ff9146cb030b9b) point ![{\displaystyle L_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0e79dc1b001f8b923df475ed14de023cbc456013) of directrix ![{\displaystyle l_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/29b25eeca673386d676f79dce674fe93040693eb) (see diagram) and focus ![{\displaystyle F_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/100c7fbf174fe8b06eacc2a6b0bb2e1badd1c7ce) are inverse with respect to the [circle inversion](/wiki/Circle_inversion "Circle inversion") at circle ![{\displaystyle x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6463e4390061fac92c5c0241ed46eb742d983ab7) (in diagram green). Hence ![{\displaystyle L_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0e79dc1b001f8b923df475ed14de023cbc456013) can be constructed as shown in the diagram. Directrix ![{\displaystyle l_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/29b25eeca673386d676f79dce674fe93040693eb) is the perpendicular to the main axis at point ![{\displaystyle L_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0e79dc1b001f8b923df475ed14de023cbc456013).

General ellipse

If the focus is ![{\displaystyle F=\left(f_{1},\,f_{2}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/119b08a584551480ae2c03dc0c3f59a32b9c902f) and the directrix ![{\displaystyle ux+vy+w=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ecd13ff1e5b131a6015fac08aae1d2635639fe34), one obtains the equation
![{\displaystyle \left(x-f_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(y-f_{2}\right)^{2}=e^{2}{\frac {\left(ux+vy+w\right)^{2}}{u^{2}+v^{2}}}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/67ff359138201bbee12bb07f0b309bb0510c5619)

(The right side of the equation uses the [Hesse normal form](/wiki/Hesse_normal_form "Hesse normal form") of a line to calculate the distance ![{\displaystyle |Pl|}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3d7c36d2d45f8cae6798b5e5f711d48ec4b7c31e).)

## Focus-to-focus reflection property

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Focus-to-focus reflection property")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Ellipse-reflex.svg/250px-Ellipse-reflex.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-reflex.svg)

Ellipse: the tangent line *w* bisects the [supplementary angle](/wiki/Supplementary_angle "Supplementary angle") L-P-F1 of the angle F1-P-F2 between the lines from point P to the foci.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Elli-norm-tang-n.svg/250px-Elli-norm-tang-n.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Elli-norm-tang-n.svg)

Rays from one focus reflect off the ellipse to pass through the other focus.

An ellipse possesses the following property:

:   The normal at a point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) bisects the angle between the lines ![{\displaystyle {\overline {PF_{1}}},\,{\overline {PF_{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cdfcc6ab1e2e9c3bfa4aee7e67bea0db349b7c13).

Proof

Because the tangent line is perpendicular to the normal, an equivalent statement is that the tangent is the external angle bisector of the lines to the foci (see diagram).
Let ![{\displaystyle L}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/103168b86f781fe6e9a4a87b8ea1cebe0ad4ede8) be the point on the line ![{\displaystyle {\overline {PF_{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65b56a3496fbba215f25fe7877e73049d9e030df) with distance ![{\displaystyle 2a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d325c24be7d760207674a169b078892bdd5cbc76) to the focus ![{\displaystyle F_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0fd17e0779153d765b40ebef91533489b87b2e37), where ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) is the semi-major axis of the ellipse. Let line ![{\displaystyle w}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/88b1e0c8e1be5ebe69d18a8010676fa42d7961e6) be the external angle bisector of the lines ![{\displaystyle {\overline {PF_{1}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1b64bd56812529ec71b4a0d441c8c7f8439477b5) and ![{\displaystyle {\overline {PF_{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/36afb04e5f31bd164611ba0bd2fb1714742a1c53) Take any other point ![{\displaystyle Q}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8752c7023b4b3286800fe3238271bbca681219ed) on ![{\displaystyle w.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d358cd6be4381ccfa44bd5702785437956d6e23f) By the [triangle inequality](/wiki/Triangle_inequality "Triangle inequality") and the [angle bisector theorem](/wiki/Angle_bisector_theorem "Angle bisector theorem"), ![{\displaystyle 2a=\left|LF_{2}\right|<{}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f9fc8c3b9edd787898e7fb1d790acb29760b8f78)![{\displaystyle \left|QF_{2}\right|+\left|QL\right|={}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2ed6a454958d51e7dd1bc66a79a1a88c388d6a41)![{\displaystyle \left|QF_{2}\right|+\left|QF_{1}\right|,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/58270708b6b9e35a23179d6132d98a022429a54a) so ![{\displaystyle Q}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8752c7023b4b3286800fe3238271bbca681219ed) must be outside the ellipse. As this is true for every choice of ![{\displaystyle Q,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d4000165887f03a10c30d085c2bf26ec3af8d5b0) ![{\displaystyle w}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/88b1e0c8e1be5ebe69d18a8010676fa42d7961e6) only intersects the ellipse at the single point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) so must be the tangent line.

Application

The rays from one focus are reflected by the ellipse to the second focus. This property has optical and acoustic applications similar to the reflective property of a parabola (see [whispering gallery](/wiki/Whispering_gallery "Whispering gallery")). It also serves to formulate [Alhazen's problem](/wiki/Alhazen%27s_problem "Alhazen's problem") of reflection on a circle tangent to the ellipse.

Additionally, because of the focus-to-focus reflection property of ellipses, if the rays are allowed to continue propagating, reflected rays will eventually align closely with the major axis.

## Conjugate diameters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Conjugate diameters")]

### Definition of conjugate diameters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Definition of conjugate diameters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Parallelproj-kreis-ellipse.svg/960px-Parallelproj-kreis-ellipse.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Parallelproj-kreis-ellipse.svg)

Orthogonal diameters of a circle with a square of tangents, midpoints of parallel chords and an affine image, which is an ellipse with conjugate diameters, a parallelogram of tangents and midpoints of chords.

Main article: [Conjugate diameters](/wiki/Conjugate_diameters "Conjugate diameters")

A circle has the following property:

:   The midpoints of parallel chords lie on a diameter.

An affine transformation preserves parallelism and midpoints of line segments, so this property is true for any ellipse. (Note that the parallel chords and the diameter are no longer orthogonal.)

Definition

Two diameters ![{\displaystyle d_{1},\,d_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8645bddab0f2c92dc5e377359ccaf2596cb99b99) of an ellipse are *conjugate* if the midpoints of chords parallel to ![{\displaystyle d_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4cccb5a6a2f1acab4ca255e0be86c224ed82282a) lie on ![{\displaystyle d_{2}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c137866b85a6b691fcfce4806943dc362bd9a889)

From the diagram one finds:

:   Two diameters ![{\displaystyle {\overline {P_{1}Q_{1}}},\,{\overline {P_{2}Q_{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/69361eed198f72d40d22a944ddf3d2d5e830e89e) of an ellipse are conjugate whenever the tangents at ![{\displaystyle P_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/398f438d75434e6fbf48dc232c1ad7228a738568) and ![{\displaystyle Q_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a8ea6463cb36d8278ff71214fb4d13127039ae53) are parallel to ![{\displaystyle {\overline {P_{2}Q_{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b6996fa0b7c2490617ddc87a27af570b273a9d16).

Conjugate diameters in an ellipse generalize orthogonal diameters in a circle.

In the parametric equation for a general ellipse given above,
![{\displaystyle {\vec {x}}={\vec {p}}(t)={\vec {f}}\!_{0}+{\vec {f}}\!_{1}\cos t+{\vec {f}}\!_{2}\sin t,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7e24778df623e258c6bac9c2cf4a0c91e14d094c)

any pair of points ![{\displaystyle {\vec {p}}(t),\ {\vec {p}}(t+\pi )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8dbfed824d30894e366dbb54b7521c5aea7d60a2) belong to a diameter, and the pair ![{\displaystyle {\vec {p}}\left(t+{\tfrac {\pi }{2}}\right),\ {\vec {p}}\left(t-{\tfrac {\pi }{2}}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/03519c46640d85feef43074a7b158eb1ebd955d2) belong to its conjugate diameter.

For the common parametric representation ![{\displaystyle (a\cos t,b\sin t)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/19e9cf86d51162f5d6f7a0fae0fcb26bce2b8739) of the ellipse with equation ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f6aa55c90da259c96a7894fe6e7555d2428cdec3) one gets: The points

:   ![{\displaystyle (x_{1},y_{1})=(\pm a\cos t,\pm b\sin t)\quad }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fb5133f6bf4df7e227e5bcd715698fe673286e6d) (signs: (+,+) or (−,−) )
:   ![{\displaystyle (x_{2},y_{2})=({\color {red}{\mp }}a\sin t,\pm b\cos t)\quad }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e12cff0f3bf763ea4ce9fb7f98e1d9b23815d721) (signs: (−,+) or (+,−) )
:   are conjugate and
:   ![{\displaystyle {\frac {x_{1}x_{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {y_{1}y_{2}}{b^{2}}}=0\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bfb16412cb2a98e9b911eff54becd028a52eacfe)

In case of a circle the last equation collapses to ![{\displaystyle x_{1}x_{2}+y_{1}y_{2}=0\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/39920839643c8611356ae9ba8e860887034f1d35)

### Theorem of Apollonios on conjugate diameters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Theorem of Apollonios on conjugate diameters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Elli-apoll-cd.svg/330px-Elli-apoll-cd.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Elli-apoll-cd.svg)

Theorem of Apollonios

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Elli-apoll-area-altern.svg/330px-Elli-apoll-area-altern.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Elli-apoll-area-altern.svg)

For the alternative area formula

For an ellipse with semi-axes ![{\displaystyle a,\,b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c12ceaff955e1a98e48db7fa8fa43121630fa209) the following is true:[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

:   Let ![{\displaystyle c_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/77b7dc6d279091d354e0b90889b463bfa7eb7247) and ![{\displaystyle c_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0b30ba1b247fb8d334580cec68561e749d24aff2) be halves of two conjugate diameters (see diagram) then

    1. ![{\displaystyle c_{1}^{2}+c_{2}^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f8027389bd601427c47af3173b2cbae03bbee9da).
    2. The *triangle* ![{\displaystyle O,P_{1},P_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/46598fcbc407dbcdd546f4432ee296e6e71c6f3b) with sides ![{\displaystyle c_{1},\,c_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e660416f603232b52217ea6e9b08e90262e6a76e) (see diagram) has the constant area ![{\textstyle A_{\Delta }={\frac {1}{2}}ab}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8c5b4601aff01982edd7e7eba36bf1e79f334fde), which can be expressed by ![{\displaystyle A_{\Delta }={\tfrac {1}{2}}c_{2}d_{1}={\tfrac {1}{2}}c_{1}c_{2}\sin \alpha }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/203f519eaab2fcb4695c95a679fc983827b3a32b), too. ![{\displaystyle d_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4cccb5a6a2f1acab4ca255e0be86c224ed82282a) is the altitude of point ![{\displaystyle P_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/398f438d75434e6fbf48dc232c1ad7228a738568) and ![{\displaystyle \alpha }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b79333175c8b3f0840bfb4ec41b8072c83ea88d3) the angle between the half diameters. Hence the area of the ellipse (see section [metric properties](#Metric_properties)) can be written as ![{\displaystyle A_{el}=\pi ab=\pi c_{2}d_{1}=\pi c_{1}c_{2}\sin \alpha }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5066aa1c60fd696aea8da312a48077d6da946046).
    3. The [parallelogram](/wiki/Parallelogram "Parallelogram") of tangents adjacent to the given conjugate diameters has the ![{\displaystyle {\text{Area}}_{12}=4ab\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5930b4f3831586188b40422fdb0d07e321a785de)

Proof

Let the ellipse be in the canonical form with parametric equation
![{\displaystyle {\vec {p}}(t)=(a\cos t,\,b\sin t).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5d015c832566e8d64f29534f7522fc12e81be871)

The two points ![{\textstyle {\vec {c}}_{1}={\vec {p}}(t),\ {\vec {c}}_{2}={\vec {p}}\left(t+{\frac {\pi }{2}}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/db5d9618868a658b682fbb27e0412ffe926123c3) are on conjugate diameters (see previous section). From trigonometric formulae one obtains ![{\displaystyle {\vec {c}}_{2}=(-a\sin t,\,b\cos t)^{\mathsf {T}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f105ead48c5e3553bcd5469876595372acc01ec0) and
![{\displaystyle \left|{\vec {c}}_{1}\right|^{2}+\left|{\vec {c}}_{2}\right|^{2}=\cdots =a^{2}+b^{2}\,.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dd10c5f90db0ab22ed9e6e944c2393ca54683f91)

The area of the triangle generated by ![{\displaystyle {\vec {c}}_{1},\,{\vec {c}}_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7c76dca8f3ae26eea9f3b0b10760d7dba129deec) is
![{\displaystyle A_{\Delta }={\tfrac {1}{2}}\det \left({\vec {c}}_{1},\,{\vec {c}}_{2}\right)=\cdots ={\tfrac {1}{2}}ab}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b42386b6d86e3170e1ecbd28063df23be1b19d97)

and from the diagram it can be seen that the area of the parallelogram is 8 times that of ![{\displaystyle A_{\Delta }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e84235d4f07db280c797a2771ff5f806b1bb5404). Hence
![{\displaystyle {\text{Area}}_{12}=4ab\,.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/238666674fef6ca91c1088b884e0747e7d8c1f92)

## Orthogonal tangents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Orthogonal tangents")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Orthoptic-ellipse-s.svg/250px-Orthoptic-ellipse-s.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Orthoptic-ellipse-s.svg)

Ellipse with its orthoptic

Main article: [Orthoptic (geometry)](/wiki/Orthoptic_(geometry) "Orthoptic (geometry)")

For the ellipse ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f6aa55c90da259c96a7894fe6e7555d2428cdec3) the intersection points of *orthogonal* tangents lie on the circle ![{\displaystyle x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}+b^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6dd31878083e5f3d3002eb9c7e1601f0b8ed698b).

This circle is called *orthoptic* or [director circle](/wiki/Director_circle "Director circle") of the ellipse (not to be confused with the circular directrix defined above).

## Drawing ellipses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Drawing ellipses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Zp-turm-tor.svg/250px-Zp-turm-tor.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Zp-turm-tor.svg)

Central projection of circles (gate)

Ellipses appear in [descriptive geometry](/wiki/Descriptive_geometry "Descriptive geometry") as images (parallel or central projection) of circles. There exist various tools to draw an ellipse. Computers provide the fastest and most accurate method for drawing an ellipse. However, technical tools (*[ellipsographs](/wiki/Ellipsograph "Ellipsograph")*) to draw an ellipse without a computer exist. The principle was known to the 5th century mathematician [Proclus](/wiki/Proclus "Proclus"), and the tool now known as an [elliptical trammel](/wiki/Elliptical_trammel "Elliptical trammel") was invented by [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

If there is no ellipsograph available, one can draw an ellipse using an [approximation by the four osculating circles at the vertices](#Approximation_by_osculating_circles).

For any method described below, knowledge of the axes and the semi-axes is necessary (or equivalently: the foci and the semi-major axis). If this presumption is not fulfilled one has to know at least two conjugate diameters. With help of [Rytz's construction](/wiki/Rytz%27s_construction "Rytz's construction") the axes and semi-axes can be retrieved.

### de La Hire's point construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: de La Hire's point construction")]

The following construction of single points of an ellipse is due to [de La Hire](/wiki/Philippe_de_La_Hire "Philippe de La Hire").[[12]](#cite_note-12) It is based on the [standard parametric representation](#Standard_parametric_representation) ![{\displaystyle (a\cos t,\,b\sin t)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a3175cd5a4697744c8c0c56109906a89fe123902) of an ellipse:

1. Draw the two *circles* centered at the center of the ellipse with radii ![{\displaystyle a,b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/181523deba732fda302fd176275a0739121d3bc8) and the axes of the ellipse.
2. Draw a *line through the center*, which intersects the two circles at point ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3) and ![{\displaystyle B}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/47136aad860d145f75f3eed3022df827cee94d7a), respectively.
3. Draw a *line* through ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3) that is parallel to the minor axis and a *line* through ![{\displaystyle B}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/47136aad860d145f75f3eed3022df827cee94d7a) that is parallel to the major axis. These lines meet at an ellipse point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) (see diagram).
4. Repeat steps (2) and (3) with different lines through the center.

* [![de La Hire's method](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Elliko-sk.svg/250px-Elliko-sk.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Elliko-sk.svg "de La Hire's method")

  de La Hire's method
* [![Animation of the method](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Parametric_ellipse.gif/250px-Parametric_ellipse.gif)](/wiki/File:Parametric_ellipse.gif "Animation of the method")

  Animation of the method

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Elliko-g.svg/250px-Elliko-g.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Elliko-g.svg)

Ellipse: gardener's method

### Pins-and-string method

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Pins-and-string method")]

The characterization of an ellipse as the locus of points so that sum of the distances to the foci is constant leads to a method of drawing one using two [drawing pins](/wiki/Drawing_pin "Drawing pin"), a length of string, and a pencil.
