In this method, pins are pushed into the paper at two points, which become the ellipse's foci. A string is tied at each end to the two pins; its length after tying is ![{\displaystyle 2a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d325c24be7d760207674a169b078892bdd5cbc76). The tip of the pencil then traces an ellipse if it is moved while keeping the string taut. Using two pegs and a rope, gardeners use this procedure to outline an elliptical flower bed—thus it is called the *gardener's ellipse*. The Byzantine architect [Anthemius of Tralles](/wiki/Anthemius_of_Tralles "Anthemius of Tralles") (c. 600) described how this method could be used to construct an elliptical reflector,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and it was elaborated in a now-lost 9th-century treatise by [Al-Ḥasan ibn Mūsā](/wiki/Al-%E1%B8%A4asan_ibn_M%C5%ABs%C4%81_ibn_Sh%C4%81kir "Al-Ḥasan ibn Mūsā ibn Shākir").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

A similar method for drawing [confocal ellipses](/wiki/Confocal_conic_sections#Graves's_theorem:_the_construction_of_confocal_ellipses_by_a_string "Confocal conic sections") with a *closed* string is due to the Irish bishop [Charles Graves](/wiki/Charles_Graves_(bishop) "Charles Graves (bishop)").

### Paper strip methods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Paper strip methods")]

The two following methods rely on the parametric representation (see *[§ Standard parametric representation](#Standard_parametric_representation)*, above):
![{\displaystyle (a\cos t,\,b\sin t)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a3175cd5a4697744c8c0c56109906a89fe123902)

This representation can be modeled technically by two simple methods. In both cases center, the axes and semi axes ![{\displaystyle a,\,b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c12ceaff955e1a98e48db7fa8fa43121630fa209) have to be known.

Method 1

The first method starts with

:   a strip of paper of length ![{\displaystyle a+b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a2391acf09244b9dba74eb940e871a6be7e7973a).

The point, where the semi axes meet is marked by ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a). If the strip slides with both ends on the axes of the desired ellipse, then point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) traces the ellipse. For the proof one shows that point ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) has the parametric representation ![{\displaystyle (a\cos t,\,b\sin t)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a3175cd5a4697744c8c0c56109906a89fe123902), where parameter ![{\displaystyle t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65658b7b223af9e1acc877d848888ecdb4466560) is the angle of the slope of the paper strip.

A technical realization of the motion of the paper strip can be achieved by a [Tusi couple](/wiki/Tusi_couple "Tusi couple") (see animation). The device is able to draw any ellipse with a *fixed* sum ![{\displaystyle a+b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a2391acf09244b9dba74eb940e871a6be7e7973a), which is the radius of the large circle. This restriction may be a disadvantage in real life. More flexible is the second paper strip method.

* [![Ellipse construction: paper strip method 1](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Elliko-pap1.svg/250px-Elliko-pap1.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Elliko-pap1.svg "Ellipse construction: paper strip method 1")

  Ellipse construction: paper strip method 1
* [![Ellipses with Tusi couple. Two examples: red and cyan.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Tusi_couple_vs_Paper_strip_plus_Ellipses_horizontal.gif/250px-Tusi_couple_vs_Paper_strip_plus_Ellipses_horizontal.gif)](/wiki/File:Tusi_couple_vs_Paper_strip_plus_Ellipses_horizontal.gif "Ellipses with Tusi couple. Two examples: red and cyan.")

  Ellipses with Tusi couple. Two examples: red and cyan.

A variation of the paper strip method 1 uses the observation that the midpoint ![{\displaystyle N}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5e3890c981ae85503089652feb48b191b57aae3) of the paper strip is moving on the circle with center ![{\displaystyle M}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f82cade9898ced02fdd08712e5f0c0151758a0dd) (of the ellipse) and radius ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {a+b}{2}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cdc7810f36b1298823a923ee35d6cd0abe79d122). Hence, the paperstrip can be cut at point ![{\displaystyle N}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5e3890c981ae85503089652feb48b191b57aae3) into halves, connected again by a joint at ![{\displaystyle N}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5e3890c981ae85503089652feb48b191b57aae3) and the sliding end ![{\displaystyle K}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2b76fce82a62ed5461908f0dc8f037de4e3686b0) fixed at the center ![{\displaystyle M}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f82cade9898ced02fdd08712e5f0c0151758a0dd) (see diagram). After this operation the movement of the unchanged half of the paperstrip is unchanged.[[15]](#cite_note-15) This variation requires only one sliding shoe.

  Variation of the paper strip method 1

Ellipse construction: paper strip method 2

Method 2

The second method starts with

:   a strip of paper of length ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc).

One marks the point, which divides the strip into two substrips of length ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3) and ![{\displaystyle a-b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1b80866c2bf2f1bc1f2e4c97e7937f5663150ea6). The strip is positioned onto the axes as described in the diagram. Then the free end of the strip traces an ellipse, while the strip is moved. For the proof, one recognizes that the tracing point can be described parametrically by ![{\displaystyle (a\cos t,\,b\sin t)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a3175cd5a4697744c8c0c56109906a89fe123902), where parameter ![{\displaystyle t}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65658b7b223af9e1acc877d848888ecdb4466560) is the angle of slope of the paper strip.

This method is the base for several *ellipsographs* (see section below).

Similar to the variation of the paper strip method 1 a *variation of the paper strip method 2* can be established (see diagram) by cutting the part between the axes into halves.

  [Elliptical trammel](/wiki/Elliptical_trammel "Elliptical trammel") (principle)

  Ellipsograph due to [Benjamin Bramer](/wiki/Benjamin_Bramer "Benjamin Bramer")
* [![Variation of the paper strip method 2](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Ellipses_with_SliderCrank_Ellipses_at_Slider_Side.gif/250px-Ellipses_with_SliderCrank_Ellipses_at_Slider_Side.gif)](/wiki/File:Ellipses_with_SliderCrank_Ellipses_at_Slider_Side.gif "Variation of the paper strip method 2")

Most ellipsograph [drafting](/wiki/Drafting_machine "Drafting machine") instruments are based on the second paperstrip method.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Elliko-skm.svg/250px-Elliko-skm.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Elliko-skm.svg)

Approximation of an ellipse with osculating circles

### Approximation by osculating circles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Approximation by osculating circles")]

From *Metric properties* below, one obtains:

* The radius of curvature at the vertices ![{\displaystyle V_{1},\,V_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/93f4c299d2482c7b9f98653c6438358f0e1334b6) is: ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {b^{2}}{a}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4b5ef2a392ae281a8fac8c9815f1a210723e93e5)
* The radius of curvature at the co-vertices ![{\displaystyle V_{3},\,V_{4}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/400b2403b01c130838cfaa31e5f208bab5923de9) is: ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {a^{2}}{b}}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ce93e1ff96829fd6766eec935cd386eb1be2d6e9)

The diagram shows an easy way to find the centers of curvature ![{\displaystyle C_{1}=\left(a-{\tfrac {b^{2}}{a}},0\right),\,C_{3}=\left(0,b-{\tfrac {a^{2}}{b}}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/091a2a449a96c70ef56a7d014af02621d6d1c34f) at vertex ![{\displaystyle V_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/adfdbc929f16cb00bb43289c223651b41f7b9f80) and co-vertex ![{\displaystyle V_{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e240a0a3bcd3a6d628a5b796501771dff86ca493), respectively:

1. mark the auxiliary point ![{\displaystyle H=(a,\,b)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/de0538c0712c31f1891512ed2fb1f9bdc6779bc7) and draw the line segment ![{\displaystyle V_{1}V_{3}\ ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/70b850fcb6948ff7d314542a7e9798321db59a41)
2. draw the line through ![{\displaystyle H}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/75a9edddcca2f782014371f75dca39d7e13a9c1b), which is perpendicular to the line ![{\displaystyle V_{1}V_{3}\ ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/70b850fcb6948ff7d314542a7e9798321db59a41)
3. the intersection points of this line with the axes are the centers of the osculating circles.

(proof: simple calculation.)

The centers for the remaining vertices are found by symmetry.

With help of a [French curve](/wiki/French_curve "French curve") one draws a curve, which has smooth contact to the [osculating circles](/wiki/Osculating_circle "Osculating circle").

### Steiner generation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Steiner generation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Ellipse-steiner-e.svg/250px-Ellipse-steiner-e.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-steiner-e.svg)

Ellipse: Steiner generation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Ellipse_construction_-_parallelogram_method.gif/250px-Ellipse_construction_-_parallelogram_method.gif)](/wiki/File:Ellipse_construction_-_parallelogram_method.gif)

Ellipse: Steiner generation

The following method to construct single points of an ellipse relies on the [Steiner generation of a conic section](/wiki/Steiner_conic "Steiner conic"):

:   Given two [pencils](/wiki/Pencil_(mathematics) "Pencil (mathematics)") ![{\displaystyle B(U),\,B(V)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/10a1643ab25d5bc87278cf445bc1c1746bfd6468) of lines at two points ![{\displaystyle U,\,V}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9afc12d08e71dfd34a8da408e9030e202b080469) (all lines containing ![{\displaystyle U}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/458a728f53b9a0274f059cd695e067c430956025) and ![{\displaystyle V}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/af0f6064540e84211d0ffe4dac72098adfa52845), respectively) and a projective but not perspective mapping ![{\displaystyle \pi }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9be4ba0bb8df3af72e90a0535fabcc17431e540a) of ![{\displaystyle B(U)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5f40b0baca9532aa8c1106155a0cae6ec77a2ce) onto ![{\displaystyle B(V)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3240b15b8d5cc8b968454438e642b397e0f4414c), then the intersection points of corresponding lines form a non-degenerate projective conic section.

For the generation of points of the ellipse ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {{\vphantom {y^{2}}}x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/15781606ff98950b1f1969011ee19bcc2767bb64) one uses the pencils at the vertices ![{\displaystyle V_{1},\,V_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/93f4c299d2482c7b9f98653c6438358f0e1334b6). Let ![{\displaystyle P=(0,\,b)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a09b79804a60cc91aad3aabeabe11d00fc92d724) be an upper co-vertex of the ellipse and ![{\displaystyle A=(-a,\,2b),\,B=(a,\,2b)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e08adf372d78010d920b57653e05906e7d5e160a).

![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) is the center of the rectangle ![{\displaystyle V_{1},\,V_{2},\,B,\,A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8c0d8884d7b07dd9cbbe6aa20ecf54eca0f1cf54). The side ![{\displaystyle {\overline {AB}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/70f91c39c977790f3cc4e768d5aad89bb1696110) of the rectangle is divided into n equal spaced line segments and this division is projected parallel with the diagonal ![{\displaystyle AV_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0ad09dac04f9df792979f555864314c2283c0a28) as direction onto the line segment ![{\displaystyle {\overline {V_{1}B}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2717941417acc1c87dfab3dae8a1721814187284) and assign the division as shown in the diagram. The parallel projection together with the reverse of the orientation is part of the projective mapping between the pencils at ![{\displaystyle V_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/adfdbc929f16cb00bb43289c223651b41f7b9f80) and ![{\displaystyle V_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ceaa689a894f5020a7b46177d201cbce2d41122b) needed. The intersection points of any two related lines ![{\displaystyle V_{1}B_{i}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/19f1f0e5d776460a59d14b0721f922e4f9d7fe8a) and ![{\displaystyle V_{2}A_{i}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f1c83b91d0029c26a60932747cb3af85f10a9e69) are points of the uniquely defined ellipse. With help of the points ![{\displaystyle C_{1},\,\dotsc }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c4ae79caeef98137b01a2b8400a26cbbb6de92cd) the points of the second quarter of the ellipse can be determined. Analogously one obtains the points of the lower half of the ellipse.

Steiner generation can also be defined for hyperbolas and parabolas. It is sometimes called a *parallelogram method* because one can use other points rather than the vertices, which starts with a parallelogram instead of a rectangle.

### As hypotrochoid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: As hypotrochoid")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Ellipse_as_hypotrochoid.gif/250px-Ellipse_as_hypotrochoid.gif)](/wiki/File:Ellipse_as_hypotrochoid.gif)

An ellipse (in red) as a special case of the [hypotrochoid](/wiki/Hypotrochoid "Hypotrochoid") with *R* = 2*r*

The ellipse is a special case of the [hypotrochoid](/wiki/Hypotrochoid "Hypotrochoid") when ![{\displaystyle R=2r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/724f22a06637c1db11f52f5e3ac84d18ac8b9073), as shown in the adjacent image. The special case of a moving circle with radius ![{\displaystyle r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0d1ecb613aa2984f0576f70f86650b7c2a132538) inside a circle with radius ![{\displaystyle R=2r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/724f22a06637c1db11f52f5e3ac84d18ac8b9073) is called a [Tusi couple](/wiki/Tusi_couple "Tusi couple").

## Inscribed angles and three-point form

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Inscribed angles and three-point form")]

### Circles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Circles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Inscribe-a-c.svg/250px-Inscribe-a-c.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Inscribe-a-c.svg)

Circle: inscribed angle theorem

A circle with equation ![{\displaystyle \left(x-x_{\circ }\right)^{2}+\left(y-y_{\circ }\right)^{2}=r^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/65b3ffcce375e21081bfb6b8e58407472bb5d437) is uniquely determined by three points ![{\displaystyle \left(x_{1},y_{1}\right),\;\left(x_{2},\,y_{2}\right),\;\left(x_{3},\,y_{3}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/27b10542291ef7a9183959ef9acd5e3e041d3d7b) not on a line. A simple way to determine the parameters ![{\displaystyle x_{\circ },y_{\circ },r}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fa918a48e56b8fa56d7f0bd7c9dee48cea9ea37e) uses the *[inscribed angle theorem](/wiki/Inscribed_angle_theorem "Inscribed angle theorem")* for circles:

:   For four points ![{\displaystyle P_{i}=\left(x_{i},\,y_{i}\right),\ i=1,\,2,\,3,\,4,\,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ed27616678ece8e69f2ac5e1372b1f522812afc9) (see diagram) the following statement is true:
:   The four points are on a circle if and only if the angles at ![{\displaystyle P_{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f92db8e65bb75b79799f0f3a29e975b37e227069) and ![{\displaystyle P_{4}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/48763cb9875de7b4125c40e95e542e775c1cbc4e) are equal.

Usually one measures inscribed angles by a degree or radian *θ*, but here the following measurement is more convenient:

:   In order to measure the angle between two lines with equations ![{\displaystyle y=m_{1}x+d_{1},\ y=m_{2}x+d_{2},\ m_{1}\neq m_{2},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e654d324c0ef45651af6cf63b62714d9f7bbb197) one uses the quotient: ![{\displaystyle {\frac {1+m_{1}m_{2}}{m_{2}-m_{1}}}=\cot \theta \ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3ade0d6e1ff5cb7363c381296fbf51c4ffebc001)

#### Inscribed angle theorem for circles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Inscribed angle theorem for circles")]

For four points ![{\displaystyle P_{i}=\left(x_{i},\,y_{i}\right),\ i=1,\,2,\,3,\,4,\,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ed27616678ece8e69f2ac5e1372b1f522812afc9) no three of them on a line, we have the following (see diagram):

:   The four points are on a circle, if and only if the angles at ![{\displaystyle P_{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f92db8e65bb75b79799f0f3a29e975b37e227069) and ![{\displaystyle P_{4}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/48763cb9875de7b4125c40e95e542e775c1cbc4e) are equal. In terms of the angle measurement above, this means: ![{\displaystyle {\frac {(x_{4}-x_{1})(x_{4}-x_{2})+(y_{4}-y_{1})(y_{4}-y_{2})}{(y_{4}-y_{1})(x_{4}-x_{2})-(y_{4}-y_{2})(x_{4}-x_{1})}}={\frac {(x_{3}-x_{1})(x_{3}-x_{2})+(y_{3}-y_{1})(y_{3}-y_{2})}{(y_{3}-y_{1})(x_{3}-x_{2})-(y_{3}-y_{2})(x_{3}-x_{1})}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/72bd9196433d9cdcb198deb8da51b40430faee8a)

At first the measure is available only for chords not parallel to the y-axis, but the final formula works for any chord.

#### Three-point form of circle equation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Three-point form of circle equation")]

:   As a consequence, one obtains an equation for the circle determined by three non-collinear points ![{\displaystyle P_{i}=\left(x_{i},\,y_{i}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/53a0cbdce93c4e2fd13de51d322bac90abdb672b): ![{\displaystyle {\frac {({\color {red}x}-x_{1})({\color {red}x}-x_{2})+({\color {red}y}-y_{1})({\color {red}y}-y_{2})}{({\color {red}y}-y_{1})({\color {red}x}-x_{2})-({\color {red}y}-y_{2})({\color {red}x}-x_{1})}}={\frac {(x_{3}-x_{1})(x_{3}-x_{2})+(y_{3}-y_{1})(y_{3}-y_{2})}{(y_{3}-y_{1})(x_{3}-x_{2})-(y_{3}-y_{2})(x_{3}-x_{1})}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/56ac3d0ff0b4380048fe5b6eaa4501c2d862aa2c)

For example, for ![{\displaystyle P_{1}=(2,\,0),\;P_{2}=(0,\,1),\;P_{3}=(0,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9a54b0ecb3ef97c1ec214667c65550ddbb29320a) the three-point equation is:

:   ![{\displaystyle {\frac {(x-2)x+y(y-1)}{yx-(y-1)(x-2)}}=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/43fd2c5c1b479e8fd095eab19630a13b36a259e0), which can be rearranged to ![{\displaystyle (x-1)^{2}+\left(y-{\tfrac {1}{2}}\right)^{2}={\tfrac {5}{4}}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dea3aa169373214dcd10547be5f3a43e2f0eb547)

Using vectors, [dot products](/wiki/Dot_product "Dot product") and [determinants](/wiki/Determinant "Determinant") this formula can be arranged more clearly, letting ![{\displaystyle {\vec {x}}=(x,\,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b9c4ac3d39f9d9ac7b2d2ad5db9e593b2f3feda5):
![{\displaystyle {\frac {\left({\color {red}{\vec {x}}}-{\vec {x}}_{1}\right)\cdot \left({\color {red}{\vec {x}}}-{\vec {x}}_{2}\right)}{\det \left({\color {red}{\vec {x}}}-{\vec {x}}_{1},{\color {red}{\vec {x}}}-{\vec {x}}_{2}\right)}}={\frac {\left({\vec {x}}_{3}-{\vec {x}}_{1}\right)\cdot \left({\vec {x}}_{3}-{\vec {x}}_{2}\right)}{\det \left({\vec {x}}_{3}-{\vec {x}}_{1},{\vec {x}}_{3}-{\vec {x}}_{2}\right)}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4146a722e1105a4207bc458df9caf73ae8313df3)

The center of the circle ![{\displaystyle \left(x_{\circ },\,y_{\circ }\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/594dd3c3be61b6829e3415a0c988565fd529e6b9) satisfies:
![{\displaystyle {\begin{bmatrix}1&{\dfrac {y_{1}-y_{2}}{x_{1}-x_{2}}}\\[2ex]{\dfrac {x_{1}-x_{3}}{y_{1}-y_{3}}}&1\end{bmatrix}}{\begin{bmatrix}x_{\circ }\\[1ex]y_{\circ }\end{bmatrix}}={\begin{bmatrix}{\dfrac {x_{1}^{2}-x_{2}^{2}+y_{1}^{2}-y_{2}^{2}}{2(x_{1}-x_{2})}}\\[2ex]{\dfrac {y_{1}^{2}-y_{3}^{2}+x_{1}^{2}-x_{3}^{2}}{2(y_{1}-y_{3})}}\end{bmatrix}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/505e489a44d7620be3ab20771d38951e0eef7409)

The radius is the distance between any of the three points and the center.
![{\displaystyle r={\sqrt {\left(x_{1}-x_{\circ }\right)^{2}+\left(y_{1}-y_{\circ }\right)^{2}}}={\sqrt {\left(x_{2}-x_{\circ }\right)^{2}+\left(y_{2}-y_{\circ }\right)^{2}}}={\sqrt {\left(x_{3}-x_{\circ }\right)^{2}+\left(y_{3}-y_{\circ }\right)^{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fe06aafa574306e2a3f1bf7debbe827699cf0b89)

### Ellipses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Ellipses")]

This section considers the family of ellipses defined by equations ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {\left(x-x_{\circ }\right)^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {\left(y-y_{\circ }\right)^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4a9aa551f5db6709e307aac624ec24f08c259196) with a *fixed* eccentricity ![{\displaystyle e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd253103f0876afc68ebead27a5aa9867d927467). It is convenient to use the parameter:
![{\displaystyle {\color {blue}q}={\frac {a^{2}}{b^{2}}}={\frac {1}{1-e^{2}}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/71c2e3cb0537c476126ced2a58236d09df4a901e)

and to write the ellipse equation as:
![{\displaystyle \left(x-x_{\circ }\right)^{2}+{\color {blue}q}\,\left(y-y_{\circ }\right)^{2}=a^{2},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/00d966c988a2fd6ae5ef9c4a0d21b8967dfda198)

where *q* is fixed and ![{\displaystyle x_{\circ },\,y_{\circ },\,a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/38cca1b465b047666cdc607a4b811d0a2bdb4cc7) vary over the real numbers. (Such ellipses have their axes parallel to the coordinate axes: if ![{\displaystyle q<1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4e1c80e174f560a6b2395c2301d6b98a67e7d624), the major axis is parallel to the *x*-axis; if ![{\displaystyle q>1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fe3606e1cf4480eb39e5ddcd46d4dae2067c0b5a), it is parallel to the *y*-axis.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Inscribe-a-e.svg/250px-Inscribe-a-e.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Inscribe-a-e.svg)

Inscribed angle theorem for an ellipse

Like a circle, such an ellipse is determined by three points not on a line.

For this family of ellipses, one introduces the following [q-analog](/wiki/Q-analog "Q-analog") angle measure, which is *not* a function of the usual angle measure *θ*:[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)

:   In order to measure an angle between two lines with equations ![{\displaystyle y=m_{1}x+d_{1},\ y=m_{2}x+d_{2},\ m_{1}\neq m_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/83237abb1040593704fd07d0a71c37aa618659d1) one uses the quotient: ![{\displaystyle {\frac {1+{\color {blue}q}\;m_{1}m_{2}}{m_{2}-m_{1}}}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/60db1f98dbe6f9608f00d4e67c5c813b9ccf79b4)

#### Inscribed angle theorem for ellipses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Inscribed angle theorem for ellipses")]

:   Given four points ![{\displaystyle P_{i}=\left(x_{i},\,y_{i}\right),\ i=1,\,2,\,3,\,4}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ed7615ec42dab17a008fc0b9252c5f7d427d7066), no three of them on a line (see diagram).
:   The four points are on an ellipse with equation ![{\displaystyle (x-x_{\circ })^{2}+{\color {blue}q}\,(y-y_{\circ })^{2}=a^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c3be8ca4ca0c3e65ae629843a7d383666c69a60b) if and only if the angles at ![{\displaystyle P_{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f92db8e65bb75b79799f0f3a29e975b37e227069) and ![{\displaystyle P_{4}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/48763cb9875de7b4125c40e95e542e775c1cbc4e) are equal in the sense of the measurement above—that is, if ![{\displaystyle {\frac {(x_{4}-x_{1})(x_{4}-x_{2})+{\color {blue}q}\;(y_{4}-y_{1})(y_{4}-y_{2})}{(y_{4}-y_{1})(x_{4}-x_{2})-(y_{4}-y_{2})(x_{4}-x_{1})}}={\frac {(x_{3}-x_{1})(x_{3}-x_{2})+{\color {blue}q}\;(y_{3}-y_{1})(y_{3}-y_{2})}{(y_{3}-y_{1})(x_{3}-x_{2})-(y_{3}-y_{2})(x_{3}-x_{1})}}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7da6aeeea28cf4476e867ce47962503c61c250c4)

At first the measure is available only for chords which are not parallel to the y-axis. But the final formula works for any chord. The proof follows from a straightforward calculation. For the direction of proof given that the points are on an ellipse, one can assume that the center of the ellipse is the origin.

#### Three-point form of ellipse equation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Three-point form of ellipse equation")]

:   A consequence, one obtains an equation for the ellipse determined by three non-collinear points ![{\displaystyle P_{i}=\left(x_{i},\,y_{i}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/53a0cbdce93c4e2fd13de51d322bac90abdb672b): ![{\displaystyle {\frac {({\color {red}x}-x_{1})({\color {red}x}-x_{2})+{\color {blue}q}\;({\color {red}y}-y_{1})({\color {red}y}-y_{2})}{({\color {red}y}-y_{1})({\color {red}x}-x_{2})-({\color {red}y}-y_{2})({\color {red}x}-x_{1})}}={\frac {(x_{3}-x_{1})(x_{3}-x_{2})+{\color {blue}q}\;(y_{3}-y_{1})(y_{3}-y_{2})}{(y_{3}-y_{1})(x_{3}-x_{2})-(y_{3}-y_{2})(x_{3}-x_{1})}}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2c762d5d0fdba9bea5a648a5f45264ba207466d1)

For example, for ![{\displaystyle P_{1}=(2,\,0),\;P_{2}=(0,\,1),\;P_{3}=(0,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9a54b0ecb3ef97c1ec214667c65550ddbb29320a) and ![{\displaystyle q=4}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7ed969e29cea24ad34d904c3574fade94cbfca08) one obtains the three-point form

:   ![{\displaystyle {\frac {(x-2)x+4y(y-1)}{yx-(y-1)(x-2)}}=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bd66bf91e60fafab041fe391517c874107d17545) and after conversion ![{\displaystyle {\frac {(x-1)^{2}}{2}}+{\frac {\left(y-{\frac {1}{2}}\right)^{2}}{\frac {1}{2}}}=1.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/531c96809a79f89af33f02b1b8b732dca89df67a)

Analogously to the circle case, the equation can be written more clearly using vectors:
![{\displaystyle {\frac {\left({\color {red}{\vec {x}}}-{\vec {x}}_{1}\right)*\left({\color {red}{\vec {x}}}-{\vec {x}}_{2}\right)}{\det \left({\color {red}{\vec {x}}}-{\vec {x}}_{1},{\color {red}{\vec {x}}}-{\vec {x}}_{2}\right)}}={\frac {\left({\vec {x}}_{3}-{\vec {x}}_{1}\right)*\left({\vec {x}}_{3}-{\vec {x}}_{2}\right)}{\det \left({\vec {x}}_{3}-{\vec {x}}_{1},{\vec {x}}_{3}-{\vec {x}}_{2}\right)}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cf1a2bb3a3f926c7864c292a662ac0e8ff815e87)

where ![{\displaystyle *}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8e9972f426d9e07855984f73ee195a21dbc21755) is the modified [dot product](/wiki/Dot_product "Dot product") ![{\displaystyle {\vec {u}}*{\vec {v}}=u_{x}v_{x}+{\color {blue}q}\,u_{y}v_{y}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d116004c9f2fae3215d430ece24d133368893d85)

## Pole-polar relation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Pole-polar relation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Ellipse-pol.svg/250px-Ellipse-pol.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipse-pol.svg)

Ellipse: pole-polar relation

Any ellipse can be described in a suitable coordinate system by an equation ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f6aa55c90da259c96a7894fe6e7555d2428cdec3). The equation of the tangent at a point ![{\displaystyle P_{1}=\left(x_{1},\,y_{1}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0b3dc7fedd66c71a7ce565744b134e669ca30ece) of the ellipse is ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x_{1}x}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y_{1}y}{b^{2}}}=1.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/91306bdbe9e1b2f3a4884a1cac67bf777b852101) If one allows point ![{\displaystyle P_{1}=\left(x_{1},\,y_{1}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0b3dc7fedd66c71a7ce565744b134e669ca30ece) to be an arbitrary point different from the origin, then

:   point ![{\displaystyle P_{1}=\left(x_{1},\,y_{1}\right)\neq (0,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/610ba12f66bced33a208f619cafa50326b0001a9) is mapped onto the line ![{\displaystyle {\tfrac {x_{1}x}{a^{2}}}+{\tfrac {y_{1}y}{b^{2}}}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0bbdaa9c0ad38b2780cbae59b7022c21145a9e0d), not through the center of the ellipse.

This relation between points and lines is a [bijection](/wiki/Bijection "Bijection").

The [inverse function](/wiki/Inverse_function "Inverse function") maps

* line ![{\displaystyle y=mx+d,\ d\neq 0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/09f2c83548a8b8b5d88d097b44ec89545db70d76) onto the point ![{\displaystyle \left(-{\tfrac {ma^{2}}{d}},\,{\tfrac {b^{2}}{d}}\right)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7ca946f6bbec65cea45fa79194170c59480afa60) and
* line ![{\displaystyle x=c,\ c\neq 0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/49d068df673bcb491813b55e60e239bce8f170e4) onto the point ![{\displaystyle \left({\tfrac {a^{2}}{c}},\,0\right).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5ee5b4fde0a5d8a065fbdccd6f084b6c62cc8c58)

Such a relation between points and lines generated by a conic is called *[pole-polar relation](/wiki/Pole_and_polar "Pole and polar")* or *polarity*. The pole is the point; the polar the line.

By calculation one can confirm the following properties of the pole-polar relation of the ellipse:

* For a point (pole) *on* the ellipse, the polar is the tangent at this point (see diagram: ![{\displaystyle P_{1},\,p_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d8c3cd1c53b94a35460d40bd3d5d9f4417085854)).
* For a pole ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) *outside* the ellipse, the intersection points of its polar with the ellipse are the tangency points of the two tangents passing ![{\displaystyle P}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b4dc73bf40314945ff376bd363916a738548d40a) (see diagram: ![{\displaystyle P_{2},\,p_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/adb6e4cdc5a5049bd23c80b840c82aa22aa35a1c)).
* For a point *within* the ellipse, the polar has no point with the ellipse in common (see diagram: ![{\displaystyle F_{1},\,l_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0ae54dde0b4f9ef9c8d609129e7ce543d30fd3c7)).

1. The intersection point of two polars is the pole of the line through their poles.
2. The foci ![{\displaystyle (c,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/21f68286402c820877c4be1ca3e0e02f4d9be445) and ![{\displaystyle (-c,\,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fd709b2dcf610893611518601114cf6c2d3e5f0c), respectively, and the directrices ![{\displaystyle x={\tfrac {a^{2}}{c}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a970d8ac8f26c51f2f3e24329cef716641e664d3) and ![{\displaystyle x=-{\tfrac {a^{2}}{c}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/856c6cd402cbd3b9598f8bf91bb8d577e380691f), respectively, belong to pairs of pole and polar. Because they are even polar pairs with respect to the circle ![{\displaystyle x^{2}+y^{2}=a^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/6463e4390061fac92c5c0241ed46eb742d983ab7), the directrices can be constructed by compass and straightedge (see [Inversive geometry](/wiki/Inversive_geometry "Inversive geometry")).

Pole-polar relations exist for hyperbolas and parabolas as well.

## Metric properties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Metric properties")]

All metric properties given below refer to an ellipse with equation

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle {\frac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}+{\frac {y^{2}}{b^{2}}}=1} |  | [1](#math_1) |

except for the section on the area enclosed by a tilted ellipse, where the generalized form of Eq.(**[1](#math_1)**) will be given.

### Area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Area")]

The [area](/wiki/Area "Area") ![{\displaystyle A_{\text{ellipse}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c6bc36f4aeab5cdea71a09c2e52da9ea17d5f42e) enclosed by an ellipse is:

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle A_{\text{ellipse}}=\pi ab} |  | [2](#math_2) |

where ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) and ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3) are the lengths of the semi-major and semi-minor axes, respectively. The area formula ![{\displaystyle \pi ab}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e49e097530aa054f7782fc7de33b0a60418ff964) is intuitive: start with a circle of radius ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3) (so its area is ![{\displaystyle \pi b^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/daae6c6582e209b2d6435533560be6f3b3cdcad1)) and stretch it by a factor ![{\displaystyle a/b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4a1b6c014398323cb45578581a536033fce1b28c) to make an ellipse. This scales the area by the same factor: ![{\displaystyle \pi b^{2}(a/b)=\pi ab.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f398e64c38c92a549220974e9ac6b5f2b581be38)[[18]](#cite_note-18) However, using the same approach for the circumference would be fallacious – compare the [integrals](/wiki/Integral "Integral") ![{\textstyle \int f(x)\,dx}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dfc65d54f82712b31932316909fb9ed3aed0e56c) and ![{\textstyle \int {\sqrt {1+f'^{2}(x)}}\,dx}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d7b1879e2c5924c6ca49e0cb679a53717e3d0a6e). It is also easy to rigorously prove the area formula using integration as follows. Equation (**[1](#math_1)**) can be rewritten as ![{\textstyle y(x)=b{\sqrt {1-x^{2}/a^{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1b50edda43a2616cc5e5ac1f4e496c26435a23ac) For ![{\displaystyle x\in [-a,a],}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fb19e5015712fa6f6c57d3f334266c73d7782434) this curve is the top half of the ellipse. So twice the integral of ![{\displaystyle y(x)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e871993bfd131a8b0c3591c26084cf8171a74dcd) over the interval ![{\displaystyle [-a,a]}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/50ccbcece37f9ec0a4c6d396be3a143a0b76d5c1) will be the area of the ellipse:
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}A_{\text{ellipse}}&=\int _{-a}^{a}2b{\sqrt {1-{\frac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}}}\,dx\\&={\frac {b}{a}}\int _{-a}^{a}2{\sqrt {a^{2}-x^{2}}}\,dx.\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5866583c9ae478df4ddf45f631b44604a40d50b1)

The second integral is the area of a circle of radius ![{\displaystyle a,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f059f053fcf9f421b7c74362cf3bd5ed024e19d1) that is, ![{\displaystyle \pi a^{2}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/582e5e8673e6fb95153672c7592026632abddc74) So
![{\displaystyle A_{\text{ellipse}}={\frac {b}{a}}\pi a^{2}=\pi ab.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/72bc01716684d17a4ca938dab82ad6e62ed51ce7)

An ellipse defined implicitly by ![{\displaystyle Ax^{2}+Bxy+Cy^{2}=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d82f2121fb477ce0d07cbf7763ba207a2827e926) has area ![{\displaystyle 2\pi /{\sqrt {4AC-B^{2}}}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c14fda4e679881a6c524bda3db37e988bc061fe)

The area can also be expressed in terms of eccentricity and the length of the semi-major axis as ![{\displaystyle a^{2}\pi {\sqrt {1-e^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/571690e2219b245c11375818d630c75fc33c7f6c) (obtained by solving for [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening"), then computing the semi-minor axis).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/TiltedEllipse2.jpg/250px-TiltedEllipse2.jpg)](/wiki/File:TiltedEllipse2.jpg)

The area enclosed by a tilted ellipse is ![{\displaystyle \pi \;y_{\text{int}}\,x_{\text{max}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/13c65f153d5693f26dedf14fba61eac3a6967c6a).

So far we have dealt with *erect* ellipses, whose major and minor axes are parallel to the ![{\displaystyle x}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/87f9e315fd7e2ba406057a97300593c4802b53e4) and ![{\displaystyle y}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b8a6208ec717213d4317e666f1ae872e00620a0d) axes. However, some applications require *tilted* ellipses. In charged-particle beam optics, for instance, the enclosed area of an erect or tilted ellipse is an important property of the beam, its *emittance*. In this case a simple formula still applies, namely

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| {\displaystyle A_{\text{ellipse}}=\pi \;y_{\text{int}}\,x_{\text{max}}=\pi \;x_{\text{int}}\,y_{\text{max}}} |  | [3](#math_3) |

where ![{\displaystyle y_{\text{int}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f3a77d5350faa21b5bf630940e5b2ef7e474b202), ![{\displaystyle x_{\text{int}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/12b182150a0353b5a85e56c8f0231d7ac3a7ac8b) are intercepts and ![{\displaystyle x_{\text{max}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f4e794819ae541eed83dc30fdd4ad7bdb5cdc988), ![{\displaystyle y_{\text{max}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f990a65e495ff47ae797bc7fb8516068b932cb2c) are maximum values. It follows directly from [Apollonios's theorem](#Theorem_of_Apollonios_on_conjugate_diameters).

### Circumference

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Circumference")]

Main article: [Perimeter of an ellipse](/wiki/Perimeter_of_an_ellipse "Perimeter of an ellipse")

Further information: [Meridian arc § Quarter meridian](/wiki/Meridian_arc#Quarter_meridian "Meridian arc")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Ellipses_same_circumference.png/250px-Ellipses_same_circumference.png)](/wiki/File:Ellipses_same_circumference.png)

Ellipses with same circumference

The circumference ![{\displaystyle C}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4fc55753007cd3c18576f7933f6f089196732029) of an ellipse is:
![{\displaystyle C\,=\,4a\int _{0}^{\pi /2}{\sqrt {1-e^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta }}\ d\theta \,=\,4a\,E(e)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4464d61cafa5ed272400d68be26dfc105e299296)

where again ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) is the length of the semi-major axis, ![{\textstyle e={\sqrt {1-b^{2}/a^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c5e1b066a3a2be6f826ae8fb20287a3f505fd940) is the eccentricity, and the function ![{\displaystyle E}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4232c9de2ee3eec0a9c0a19b15ab92daa6223f9b) is the [complete elliptic integral of the second kind](/wiki/Complete_elliptic_integral_of_the_second_kind "Complete elliptic integral of the second kind"),
![{\displaystyle E(e)\,=\,\int _{0}^{\pi /2}{\sqrt {1-e^{2}\sin ^{2}\theta }}\ d\theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bcb6775742c535b22be98e46c57745816fea2742)
which is in general not an [elementary function](/wiki/Elementary_function "Elementary function").

The circumference of the ellipse may be evaluated in terms of ![{\displaystyle E(e)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e8c3c60376e68f0651f4f927633006aadb64601e) using [Gauss's arithmetic-geometric mean](/wiki/Arithmetic-geometric_mean "Arithmetic-geometric mean");[[19]](#cite_note-19) this is a quadratically converging [iterative method](/wiki/Iterative_method "Iterative method") (see [here](/wiki/Elliptic_integral#Computation "Elliptic integral") for details).

The exact [infinite series](/wiki/Infinite_series "Infinite series") is:
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\frac {C}{2\pi a}}&=1-\left({\frac {1}{2}}\right)^{2}e^{2}-\left({\frac {1\cdot 3}{2\cdot 4}}\right)^{2}{\frac {e^{4}}{3}}-\left({\frac {1\cdot 3\cdot 5}{2\cdot 4\cdot 6}}\right)^{2}{\frac {e^{6}}{5}}-\cdots \\&=1-\sum _{n=1}^{\infty }\left({\frac {(2n-1)!!}{(2n)!!}}\right)^{2}{\frac {e^{2n}}{2n-1}}\\&=-\sum _{n=0}^{\infty }\left({\frac {(2n-1)!!}{(2n)!!}}\right)^{2}{\frac {e^{2n}}{2n-1}},\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fc89ffe697adef54905ae3b133a6870d63882b0a)
where ![{\displaystyle n!!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/511717d541dba5357928e8d8631f1b4d4f8d5b31) is the [double factorial](/wiki/Double_factorial "Double factorial") (extended to negative odd integers in the usual way, giving ![{\displaystyle (-1)!!=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/399bb0701ee77817613b0d3b197076f210923130) and ![{\displaystyle (-3)!!=-1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/188a2d464a574aa2c61a21610fc7aba1228bd05a)).

This series converges, but by expanding in terms of ![{\displaystyle h=(a-b)^{2}/(a+b)^{2},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/16a34465dd9ec33947b0be74eb173ebb86dc21ca) [James Ivory](/wiki/James_Ivory_(mathematician) "James Ivory (mathematician)"),[[20]](#cite_note-20) [Bessel](/wiki/Friedrich_Wilhelm_Bessel "Friedrich Wilhelm Bessel")[[21]](#cite_note-21) and [Kummer](/wiki/Ernst_Kummer "Ernst Kummer")[[22]](#cite_note-Linderholm95-22) derived a series that converges much more rapidly. It is most concisely written in terms of the [binomial coefficient with ![{\displaystyle n=1/2}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c1721524d24b6231bdffb2befeef0f3c4e021f96)](/wiki/Binomial_coefficient#Binomial_coefficient_with_n_=_1/2 "Binomial coefficient"):
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}{\frac {C}{\pi (a+b)}}&=\sum _{n=0}^{\infty }{{\frac {1}{2}} \choose n}^{2}h^{n}\\&=\sum _{n=0}^{\infty }\left({\frac {(2n-3)!!}{(2n)!!}}\right)^{2}h^{n}\\&=\sum _{n=0}^{\infty }\left({\frac {(2n-3)!!}{2^{n}n!}}\right)^{2}h^{n}\\&=\sum _{n=0}^{\infty }\left({\frac {1}{(2n-1)4^{n}}}{\binom {2n}{n}}\right)^{2}h^{n}\\&=1+{\frac {h}{4}}+{\frac {h^{2}}{64}}+{\frac {h^{3}}{256}}+{\frac {25\,h^{4}}{16384}}+{\frac {49\,h^{5}}{65536}}+{\frac {441\,h^{6}}{2^{20}}}+{\frac {1089\,h^{7}}{2^{22}}}+\cdots .\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2929ad69c32ee6de5afdd4a2181a6bd1053b1627)
The coefficients are slightly smaller (by a factor of ![{\displaystyle 2n-1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fb6ce8a02613283b1e60305814a1457335b44437)), but also ![{\displaystyle e^{4}/16\leq h\leq e^{4}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/243cf1ccda25778bb45ce74c505e49350f9e5ccd) is numerically much smaller than ![{\displaystyle e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd253103f0876afc68ebead27a5aa9867d927467) except at ![{\displaystyle h=e=0}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a3898938a5e940cc810cd7a400b83e39f0e57afc) and ![{\displaystyle h=e=1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5eb775288cb14731716161964e71ecd1c8a26462). For eccentricities less than 0.5 (![{\displaystyle h<0.005}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/57e6adcecb83826b2ad7afc3ad5000d7ea61705c)), the error is at the limits of [double-precision floating-point](/wiki/Double-precision_floating-point "Double-precision floating-point") after the ![{\displaystyle h^{4}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/21db4c317590e3525f8ea8ad31f455d7af8ee8f3) term.[[23]](#cite_note-Cook23-23)

[Srinivasa Ramanujan](/wiki/Srinivasa_Ramanujan "Srinivasa Ramanujan") gave two close [approximations](/wiki/Approximations "Approximations") for the circumference in §16 of "Modular Equations and Approximations to ![{\displaystyle \pi }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9be4ba0bb8df3af72e90a0535fabcc17431e540a)";[[24]](#cite_note-24) they are
![{\displaystyle {\frac {C}{\pi }}\approx 3(a+b)-{\sqrt {(3a+b)(a+3b)}}=3(a+b)-{\sqrt {3(a+b)^{2}+4ab}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/1ed9d937987468a32a6c77fd72a10dea6de13cdf)
and
![{\displaystyle {\frac {C}{\pi (a+b)}}\approx 1+{\frac {3h}{10+{\sqrt {4-3h}}}},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/33f906d08368e33bef473d213f11d920e7c7cac7)
where ![{\displaystyle h}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b26be3e694314bc90c3215047e4a2010c6ee184a) takes on the same meaning as above. The errors in these approximations, which were obtained empirically, are of order ![{\displaystyle h^{3}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/89571cb3cd52b5c1a859ab7035c7b94e85b37149) and ![{\displaystyle h^{5},}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0e8e8b4dc870b8d12b7570f46d0fb5d33b508837) respectively.[[25]](#cite_note-Villarino-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) This is because the second formula's infinite series expansion matches Ivory's formula up to the ![{\displaystyle h^{4}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/21db4c317590e3525f8ea8ad31f455d7af8ee8f3) term.[[25]](#cite_note-Villarino-25): 3

### Arc length

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Arc length")]

Further information: [Meridian arc § Calculation](/wiki/Meridian_arc#Calculation "Meridian arc")

More generally, the [arc length](/wiki/Arc_length "Arc length") of a portion of the circumference, as a function of the angle subtended (or x coordinates of any two points on the upper half of the ellipse), is given by an incomplete [elliptic integral](/wiki/Elliptic_integral "Elliptic integral"). The upper half of an ellipse is parameterized by
![{\displaystyle y=b\ {\sqrt {1-{\frac {x^{2}}{a^{2}}}\ }}~.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bc73a946358be5cf71e101d9eab7671624a13c2c)

Then the arc length ![{\displaystyle s}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/01d131dfd7673938b947072a13a9744fe997e632) from ![{\displaystyle \ x_{1}\ }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c0dc5498fcfdce482df865b038fce68ba2feccce) to ![{\displaystyle \ x_{2}\ }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/06b46c780023ceb9e8fa2f18efdf15b4fb1a5846) is:
![{\displaystyle s=-b\int _{\arccos {\frac {x_{1}}{a}}}^{\arccos {\frac {x_{2}}{a}}}{\sqrt {\ 1+\left({\tfrac {a^{2}}{b^{2}}}-1\right)\ \sin ^{2}z~}}\;dz~.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/74c775a3bc122079113dcfbbdcf96a3b504726a4)

This is equivalent to
![{\displaystyle s=b\ \left[\;E\left(z\;{\Biggl |}\;1-{\frac {a^{2}}{b^{2}}}\right)\;\right]_{z\ =\ \arccos {\frac {x_{2}}{a}}}^{\arccos {\frac {x_{1}}{a}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b7338c6c7bf9cc8e20da6c7da90eecd93f540416)

where ![{\displaystyle E(z\mid m)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bfce24564681cfb65893ff902a35c704e35b117e) is the incomplete elliptic integral of the second kind with parameter ![{\displaystyle m=k^{2}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9aa9dfc5de102aa62bdef3bccae9fd22f395a76e)

Some lower and upper bounds on the circumference of the canonical ellipse ![{\displaystyle \ x^{2}/a^{2}+y^{2}/b^{2}=1\ }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ce98ef6e6a5897b86d8fcc2f07f2a5931b115253) with ![{\displaystyle \ a\geq b\ }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0289b5a1b727289a3e5bcaa1cadff5afb4e8d173) are[[27]](#cite_note-27)
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}2\pi b&\leq C\leq 2\pi a\ ,\\\pi (a+b)&\leq C\leq 4(a+b)\ ,\\4{\sqrt {a^{2}+b^{2}\ }}&\leq C\leq {\sqrt {2\ }}\pi {\sqrt {a^{2}+b^{2}\ }}~.\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/10c51daa48e15b4128d882ef9357b283a36a06fc)

Here the upper bound ![{\displaystyle \ 2\pi a\ }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0b26d243ecbedb16b901785c0c0bd6e8b8374966) is the circumference of a [circumscribed](/wiki/Circumscribed_circle "Circumscribed circle") [concentric circle](/wiki/Concentric_circle "Concentric circle") passing through the endpoints of the ellipse's major axis, and the lower bound ![{\displaystyle 4{\sqrt {a^{2}+b^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e33305ba73b323001753e068840c1c1224d58638) is the perimeter of an [inscribed](/wiki/Inscribed_figure "Inscribed figure") [rhombus](/wiki/Rhombus "Rhombus") with [vertices](/wiki/Vertex_(geometry) "Vertex (geometry)") at the endpoints of the major and the minor axes.

Given an ellipse whose axes are drawn, we can construct the endpoints of a particular elliptic arc whose length is one eighth of the ellipse's circumference using only [straightedge and compass](/wiki/Straightedge_and_compass_construction "Straightedge and compass construction") in a finite number of steps; for some specific shapes of ellipses, such as when the axes have a length ratio of ⁠![{\displaystyle {\sqrt {2}}:1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fd734a2303c444bca42418ab6fc306645e2f596c)⁠, it is additionally possible to construct the endpoints of a particular arc whose length is one twelfth of the circumference.[[28]](#cite_note-28) (The vertices and co-vertices are already endpoints of arcs whose length is one half or one quarter of the ellipse's circumference.) However, the general theory of straightedge-and-compass elliptic division appears to be unknown, unlike in [the case of the circle](/wiki/Constructible_polygon "Constructible polygon") and [the lemniscate](/wiki/Lemniscate_elliptic_functions "Lemniscate elliptic functions"). The division in special cases has been investigated by [Legendre](/wiki/Adrien-Marie_Legendre "Adrien-Marie Legendre") in his classical treatise.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Curvature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Curvature")]

The [curvature](/wiki/Curvature "Curvature") is given by:

![{\displaystyle \kappa ={\frac {1}{a^{2}b^{2}}}\left({\frac {x^{2}}{a^{4}}}+{\frac {y^{2}}{b^{4}}}\right)^{-{\frac {3}{2}}}\ ,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d3749c0eabde6f1a53cea17700f1ac2d34207a32)

and the [radius of curvature](/wiki/Radius_of_curvature#Ellipses "Radius of curvature"), ρ = 1/κ, at point ![{\displaystyle (x,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/41cf50e4a314ca8e2c30964baa8d26e5be7a9386):
![{\displaystyle \rho =a^{2}b^{2}\left({\frac {x^{2}}{a^{4}}}+{\frac {y^{2}}{b^{4}}}\right)^{\frac {3}{2}}={\frac {1}{a^{4}b^{4}}}{\sqrt {\left(a^{4}y^{2}+b^{4}x^{2}\right)^{3}}}\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5106c69d25f8bc334081befaf1c0f26f6d4a314a)The radius of curvature of an ellipse, as a function of angle *θ* from the center, is:
![{\displaystyle R(\theta )={\frac {a^{2}}{b}}{\biggl (}{\frac {1-e^{2}(2-e^{2})(\cos \theta )^{2})}{1-e^{2}(\cos \theta )^{2}}}{\biggr )}^{3/2}\,,}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9dd712f3c842adba24d8f562bfa400c4867027c8)where e is the eccentricity.

Radius of curvature at the two *vertices* ![{\displaystyle (\pm a,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/58f43d4d49576077a52ce7939e40b34e831b5fa5) and the centers of curvature:
![{\displaystyle \rho _{0}={\frac {b^{2}}{a}}=p\ ,\qquad \left(\pm {\frac {c^{2}}{a}}\,{\bigg |}\,0\right)\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4557a5f894c2354243e334b2cc6bc326dc8d2fe6)

Radius of curvature at the two *co-vertices* ![{\displaystyle (0,\pm b)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/c4a4b6f543f2c7d7fbb812baa5306a5a9ced8c21) and the centers of curvature:
![{\displaystyle \rho _{1}={\frac {a^{2}}{b}}\ ,\qquad \left(0\,{\bigg |}\,\pm {\frac {c^{2}}{b}}\right)\ .}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e1b5ed0360c73ff3a5ad02af4bdbf7c46490b6de)The locus of all the centers of curvature is called an [evolute](/wiki/Evolute#Evolute_of_an_ellipse "Evolute"). In the case of an ellipse, the evolute is an [astroid](/wiki/Astroid "Astroid").

## In triangle geometry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: In triangle geometry")]

Ellipses appear in triangle geometry as

1. [Steiner ellipse](/wiki/Steiner_ellipse "Steiner ellipse"): ellipse through the vertices of the triangle with center at the centroid,
2. [inellipses](/wiki/Inellipse "Inellipse"): ellipses which touch the sides of a triangle. Special cases are the [Steiner inellipse](/wiki/Steiner_inellipse "Steiner inellipse") and the [Mandart inellipse](/wiki/Mandart_inellipse "Mandart inellipse").

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Applications")]

### Physics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Physics")]

#### Elliptical reflectors and acoustics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Elliptical reflectors and acoustics")]

See also: [Fresnel zone](/wiki/Fresnel_zone "Fresnel zone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/%22Wave_pattern_of_a_little_droplet_dropped_into_mercury_in_one_focus_of_the_ellipse_%22_by_Weber_Bros..jpg/250px-%22Wave_pattern_of_a_little_droplet_dropped_into_mercury_in_one_focus_of_the_ellipse_%22_by_Weber_Bros..jpg)](/wiki/File:%22Wave_pattern_of_a_little_droplet_dropped_into_mercury_in_one_focus_of_the_ellipse_%22_by_Weber_Bros..jpg)

Wave pattern of a little droplet dropped into mercury in the foci of the ellipse

If the water's surface is disturbed at one focus of an elliptical water tank, the circular waves of that disturbance, after [reflecting](/wiki/Reflection_(physics) "Reflection (physics)") off the walls, converge simultaneously to a single point: the *second focus*. This is a consequence of the total travel length being the same along any wall-bouncing path between the two foci.

Similarly, if a light source is placed at one focus of an elliptic [mirror](/wiki/Mirror "Mirror"), all light rays on the plane of the ellipse are reflected to the second focus. Since no other smooth curve has such a property, it can be used as an alternative definition of an ellipse. (In the special case of a circle with a source at its center all light would be reflected back to the center.) If the ellipse is rotated along its major axis to produce an ellipsoidal mirror (specifically, a [prolate spheroid](/wiki/Prolate_spheroid "Prolate spheroid")), this property holds for all rays out of the source. Alternatively, a cylindrical mirror with elliptical cross-section can be used to focus light from a linear [fluorescent lamp](/wiki/Fluorescent_lamp "Fluorescent lamp") along a line of the paper; such mirrors are used in some [document scanners](/wiki/Image_scanner "Image scanner").

Sound waves are reflected in a similar way, so in a large elliptical room a person standing at one focus can hear a person standing at the other focus remarkably well. The effect is even more evident under a [vaulted roof](/wiki/Cupola "Cupola") shaped as a section of a prolate spheroid. Such a room is called a *[whisper chamber](/wiki/Whisper_chamber "Whisper chamber")*. The same effect can be demonstrated with two reflectors shaped like the end caps of such a spheroid, placed facing each other at the proper distance. Examples are the [National Statuary Hall](/wiki/National_Statuary_Hall "National Statuary Hall") at the [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol") (where [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams") is said to have used this property for eavesdropping on political matters); the [Mormon Tabernacle](/wiki/Mormon_Tabernacle "Mormon Tabernacle") at [Temple Square](/wiki/Temple_Square "Temple Square") in [Salt Lake City](/wiki/Salt_Lake_City "Salt Lake City"), [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"); at an exhibit on sound at the [Museum of Science and Industry](/wiki/Museum_of_Science_and_Industry_(Chicago) "Museum of Science and Industry (Chicago)") in [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"); in front of the [University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign](/wiki/University_of_Illinois_at_Urbana%E2%80%93Champaign "University of Illinois at Urbana–Champaign") Foellinger Auditorium; and also at a side chamber of the Palace of Charles V, in the [Alhambra](/wiki/Alhambra "Alhambra").

#### Planetary orbits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Planetary orbits")]

Main article: [Elliptic orbit](/wiki/Elliptic_orbit "Elliptic orbit")

In the 17th century, [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler") discovered that the orbits along which the planets travel around the Sun are ellipses with the Sun [approximately] at one focus, in his [first law of planetary motion](/wiki/Kepler%27s_laws_of_planetary_motion "Kepler's laws of planetary motion"). Later, [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") explained this as a corollary of his [law of universal gravitation](/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation "Newton's law of universal gravitation").

More generally, in the gravitational [two-body problem](/wiki/Two-body_problem "Two-body problem"), if the two bodies are bound to each other (that is, the total energy is negative), their orbits are [similar](/wiki/Similarity_(geometry) "Similarity (geometry)") ellipses with the common [barycenter](/wiki/Barycenter "Barycenter") being one of the foci of each ellipse. The other focus of either ellipse has no known physical significance. The orbit of either body in the reference frame of the other is also an ellipse, with the other body at the same focus.

Keplerian elliptical orbits are the result of any radially directed attraction force whose strength is inversely proportional to the square of the distance. Thus, in principle, the motion of two oppositely charged particles in empty space would also be an ellipse. (However, this conclusion ignores losses due to [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation") and [quantum effects](/wiki/Quantum_mechanics "Quantum mechanics"), which become significant when the particles are moving at high speed.)

For [elliptical orbits](/wiki/Elliptical_orbit "Elliptical orbit"), useful relations involving the eccentricity ![{\displaystyle e}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/cd253103f0876afc68ebead27a5aa9867d927467) are:
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}e&={\frac {r_{a}-r_{p}}{r_{a}+r_{p}}}={\frac {r_{a}-r_{p}}{2a}}\\r_{a}&=(1+e)a\\r_{p}&=(1-e)a\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a77dbb190ea1e2efdf61a8c866df331d463081b0)

where

* ![{\displaystyle r_{a}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/da5f0781a46ba20129a554237056b6ade78b956f) is the radius at [apoapsis](/wiki/Apoapsis "Apoapsis"), i.e., the farthest distance of the orbit to the [barycenter](/wiki/Barycenter "Barycenter") of the system, which is a [focus](/wiki/Focus_(geometry) "Focus (geometry)") of the ellipse
* ![{\displaystyle r_{p}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d02ec163fac8837ac757005d783b375e52808b97) is the radius at [periapsis](/wiki/Periapsis "Periapsis"), the closest distance
* ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) is the length of the [semi-major axis](/wiki/Semi-major_axis "Semi-major axis")

Also, in terms of ![{\displaystyle r_{a}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/da5f0781a46ba20129a554237056b6ade78b956f) and ![{\displaystyle r_{p}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d02ec163fac8837ac757005d783b375e52808b97), the semi-major axis ![{\displaystyle a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ffd2487510aa438433a2579450ab2b3d557e5edc) is their [arithmetic mean](/wiki/Arithmetic_mean "Arithmetic mean"), the semi-minor axis ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3) is their [geometric mean](/wiki/Geometric_mean "Geometric mean"), and the [semi-latus rectum](/wiki/Conic_section#Features "Conic section") ![{\displaystyle \ell }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f066e981e530bacc07efc6a10fa82deee985929e) is their [harmonic mean](/wiki/Harmonic_mean "Harmonic mean"). In other words,
![{\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}a&={\frac {r_{a}+r_{p}}{2}}\\[2pt]b&={\sqrt {r_{a}r_{p}}}\\[2pt]\ell &={\frac {2}{{\frac {1}{r_{a}}}+{\frac {1}{r_{p}}}}}={\frac {2r_{a}r_{p}}{r_{a}+r_{p}}}.\end{aligned}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fe971ae54d15192d057cf4ab90494745bfb3a006)

#### Harmonic oscillators

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Harmonic oscillators")]

The general solution for a [harmonic oscillator](/wiki/Harmonic_oscillator "Harmonic oscillator") in two or more [dimensions](/wiki/Dimension "Dimension") is also an ellipse. Such is the case, for instance, of a long pendulum that is free to move in two dimensions; of a mass attached to a fixed point by a perfectly elastic [spring](/wiki/Spring_(mechanics) "Spring (mechanics)"); or of any object that moves under influence of an attractive force that is directly proportional to its distance from a fixed attractor. Unlike Keplerian orbits, however, these "harmonic orbits" have the center of attraction at the geometric center of the ellipse, and have fairly simple equations of motion.

#### Phase visualization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Phase visualization")]

In [electronics](/wiki/Electronics "Electronics"), the relative phase of two sinusoidal signals can be compared by feeding them to the vertical and horizontal inputs of an [oscilloscope](/wiki/Oscilloscope "Oscilloscope"). If the [Lissajous figure](/wiki/Lissajous_figure "Lissajous figure") display is an ellipse, rather than a straight line, the two signals are out of phase.

#### Elliptical gears

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Elliptical gears")]

Two [non-circular gears](/wiki/Non-circular_gear "Non-circular gear") with the same elliptical outline, each pivoting around one focus and positioned at the proper angle, turn smoothly while maintaining contact at all times. Alternatively, they can be connected by a [link chain](/wiki/Link_chain "Link chain") or [timing belt](/wiki/Toothed_belt "Toothed belt"), or in the case of a bicycle the main [chainring](/wiki/Chainwheel#ovoid_chainwheels "Chainwheel") may be elliptical, or an [ovoid](/wiki/Ovoid "Ovoid") similar to an ellipse in form. Such elliptical gears may be used in mechanical equipment to produce variable [angular speed](/wiki/Angular_speed "Angular speed") or [torque](/wiki/Torque "Torque") from a constant rotation of the driving axle, or in the case of a bicycle to allow a varying crank rotation speed with inversely varying [mechanical advantage](/wiki/Mechanical_advantage "Mechanical advantage").

Elliptical bicycle gears make it easier for the chain to slide off the cog when changing gears.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

An example gear application would be a device that winds thread onto a conical [bobbin](/wiki/Bobbin "Bobbin") on a [spinning](/wiki/Spinning_(textiles) "Spinning (textiles)") machine. The bobbin would need to wind faster when the thread is near the apex than when it is near the base.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

#### Optics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Optics")]

* In a material that is optically [anisotropic](/wiki/Anisotropic "Anisotropic") ([birefringent](/wiki/Birefringent "Birefringent")), the [refractive index](/wiki/Refractive_index "Refractive index") depends on the direction of the light. The dependency can be described by an [index ellipsoid](/wiki/Index_ellipsoid "Index ellipsoid"). (If the material is optically [isotropic](/wiki/Isotropic "Isotropic"), this ellipsoid is a sphere.)
* In lamp-[pumped](/wiki/Laser_pumping "Laser pumping") solid-state lasers, elliptical cylinder-shaped reflectors have been used to direct light from the pump lamp (coaxial with one ellipse focal axis) to the active medium rod (coaxial with the second focal axis).[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* In laser-plasma produced [EUV](/wiki/Extreme_ultraviolet "Extreme ultraviolet") light sources used in microchip [lithography](/wiki/Extreme_ultraviolet_lithography "Extreme ultraviolet lithography"), EUV light is generated by plasma positioned in the primary focus of an ellipsoid mirror and is collected in the secondary focus at the input of the lithography machine.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Statistics and finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Statistics and finance")]

In [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics"), a bivariate [random vector](/wiki/Random_vector "Random vector") ![{\displaystyle (X,Y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/41f29b9537685f499713112d6802e811cbf51bba) is [jointly elliptically distributed](/wiki/Elliptical_distribution "Elliptical distribution") if its iso-density contours—loci of equal values of the density function—are ellipses. The concept extends to an arbitrary number of elements of the random vector, in which case in general the iso-density contours are ellipsoids. A special case is the [multivariate normal distribution](/wiki/Multivariate_normal_distribution "Multivariate normal distribution"). The elliptical distributions are important in the financial field because if rates of return on assets are jointly elliptically distributed then all portfolios can be characterized completely by their mean and variance—that is, any two portfolios with identical mean and variance of portfolio return have identical distributions of portfolio return.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Computer graphics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Computer graphics")]

Drawing an ellipse as a [graphics primitive](/wiki/Graphics_primitive "Graphics primitive") is common in standard display libraries, such as the MacIntosh [QuickDraw](/wiki/QuickDraw "QuickDraw") API, and [Direct2D](/wiki/Direct2D "Direct2D") on Windows. [Jack Bresenham](/wiki/Jack_Bresenham "Jack Bresenham") at IBM is most famous for the invention of 2D drawing primitives, including line and circle drawing, using only fast integer operations such as addition and branch on carry bit. M. L. V. Pitteway extended Bresenham's algorithm for lines to conics in 1967.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Another efficient generalization to draw ellipses was invented in 1984 by Jerry Van Aken.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In 1970 Danny Cohen presented at the "Computer Graphics 1970" conference in England a linear algorithm for drawing ellipses and circles. In 1971, L. B. Smith published similar algorithms for all conic sections and proved them to have good properties.[[38]](#cite_note-38) These algorithms need only a few multiplications and additions to calculate each vector.

It is beneficial to use a parametric formulation in computer graphics because the density of points is greatest where there is the most curvature. Thus, the change in slope between each successive point is small, reducing the apparent "jaggedness" of the approximation.

Drawing with Bézier paths

[Composite Bézier curves](/wiki/Composite_B%C3%A9zier_curve "Composite Bézier curve") may also be used to draw an ellipse to sufficient accuracy, since any ellipse may be construed as an [affine transformation](/wiki/Affine_transformation "Affine transformation") of a circle. The spline methods used to draw a circle may be used to draw an ellipse, since the constituent [Bézier curves](/wiki/B%C3%A9zier_curve "Bézier curve") behave appropriately under such transformations.

### Optimization theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ellipse&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: Optimization theory")]

It is sometimes useful to find the minimum bounding ellipse on a set of points. The [ellipsoid method](/wiki/Ellipsoid_method "Ellipsoid method") is quite useful for solving this problem.
